segments.s666-g57: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when.' فرمان دهد is a classical present/present-subjunctive-style clause 'gives an order' (فرمان = 'order' as object, دهد = 'gives'). نامبردار شاه is the subject phrase 'the renowned king' (adjective + noun), placed after the verb in the older/poetic word order.
segments.s666-g58: منم is the emphatic copula-like 'I am' (من + ـم). ساخته is a past participle 'made' used resultatively (passive/causative sense: 'have been made' or 'they made'), and پهلوان سپاه is a genitive/construct phrase 'champion of the army' (پهلوان = 'champion', modified by سپاه = 'army').
segments.s666-g59: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they departed.' بیدار کارآگهان is a noun phrase 'the awake/alert, knowledgeable ones' (بیدار as adjective 'alert', کارآگهان 'the informed/aware ones'); classical word order places the verb before its subject.
segments.s666-g60: هم آنگه is a connective adverbial 'also/thereupon/then likewise' linking the next action. بر is the directional preposition 'to/toward,' and شهریار جهان is a title phrase 'the sovereign/king of the world' (شهریار = 'king', جهان = 'world').
segments.s666-g61: سخن‌های بهرام is a genitive phrase 'the words/speeches of Bahram' (سخن‌های = plural 'words/speeches'). چندانک is a degree adverb 'to that extent/so much,' and بود is the past copula 'were/was' — together: 'Bahram's words were so many.'
segments.s666-g62: بهر + یک سراینده is a measure phrase 'for each/for one poet' (بهر = 'for/to', یک سراینده = 'one poet'). ده is the numeral 'ten' (the thing given), and برفزود is a past verb 'bestowed/added' — literal: 'he bestowed ten for each poet.'
segments.s666-g63: شهنشاه ایران is the subject 'the emperor/sovereign of Iran.' ازان is an adverbial 'from that/thereupon' referring back to the preceding events, and شاد شد is a past-resultative phrase 'became glad' ('شاد' = happy, شد = became).
segments.s666-g64: ز تیمار indicates separation 'from the care/burden' (ز = 'from', تیمار = 'care/charge'); آن لشکر 'that army' is the source of that care, and آزاد شد is the passive/result 'was freed' (آزاد = free, شد = became/was). Together: 'was freed from the charge of that army.'
segments.s666-g257: بدو (به + او) is the dative/indirect-object combination 'to him' placed before the verb in older/poetic Persian; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here omitted from the provided sense list); تیز is an adverb 'quick(ly)' modifying the command; از پس پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'after/from behind the champion' (از + پس = 'from behind/after' with پهلوان as the object).
segments.s666-g258: برو is an imperative 'go'; تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that') and takes a subjunctive, here بینی 'you may see'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; به من بر‌بخوان is an imperative instruction 'call/announce it to me' (برخوان used as 'announce/call out' to the speaker).
segments.s666-g259: بیامد is past 'came'; سخنگوی پویان is a noun phrase 'the spokesman/speaker of the poyān' where پویان is an epithet meaning 'active/fast' (here describing the speaker); ز پس is the poetic preposition 'from behind/after' indicating his direction of approach.
segments.s666-g260: نبود is the negative past 'there was not/wasn't'; آگه از کار او هیچکس is the remainder: 'no one was aware (آگه) of (از) his (او) deeds/affairs (کار)'. Word order places the verb before the negated predicate phrase, typical of older Persian.
segments.s666-g261: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking back to the antecedent; هم ... و هم ... is the correlative 'both ... and ...'; راهبر بود is 'was a guide/leader' and فال‌گوی 'a fortune‑teller/one who reads omens' completes the apposition.
segments.s666-g262: سرانجام هر کار is a noun phrase 'the outcome/end of every affair' (سرانجام = outcome, هر کار = every business/affair). گفتی here functions as a nominalized 'saying/report' (what is said) and بدوی ('original/old') qualifies that saying—together meaning he prognosticated/declared the outcome of each matter in the customary/old manner.
segments.s666-g263: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بهرام is the subject; بیرون شد از طیسفون = 'went out of Ctesiphon' where شد functions as the past auxiliary/verb ('went/was made to go') and از indicates origin ('from').
segments.s666-g264: همی‌راند is the imperfect/progressive past with the prefix همی- 'was riding/was charging'; با is the instrumental 'with' (holding/using), نیزه is 'spear', and پیش اندرون indicates direction 'toward the inner court/inside', i.e. he rode in with spear before him.
segments.s666-g65: ورا (an enclitic or unstressed pronoun form) means 'to him/it' and refers back to a previously mentioned person; کرد is the simple past of کردن 'to do/make' so the clause literally 'made him'; سالار is the subject 'the commander'. The prepositional phrase بر لشکرش follows and marks what he made him over — 'over his army' (بر = over/against, لشکرش = 'his army' with the possessive clitic ش). Word order is verb (کرد) with pronoun/object (ورا) before it, then subject, then the prepositional complement.
segments.s666-g66: آورد is the past of آوردن 'to bring' and is used transitively; اندر 'in/into' marks motion into the army. بابر is a proper name (here introduced as the person brought in). جنگی functions as a descriptive noun/adjective 'warlike / a warrior', and سرش 'its/his head' uses سر = 'head/leader' plus the clitic ش = 'his/its', so the phrase means 'bringing Bābar in, making a warrior the head of it.'
segments.s666-g67: هرآنکس که 'whoever that' is a relative/indefinite phrase; جست is an older past or perfective of جستن 'to seek' and here means 'sought' (i.e. sought fame). از یلان 'from among the heroes' uses از = 'from/out of' and یلان = 'the heroes'. نام را uses نام 'name/reputation' with the object marker را, meaning 'a name' or 'fame' as the object of جست.
segments.s666-g68: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a military title). همی‌خواند is an archaic/imperfective form (همی- + خواند) meaning 'kept reciting/named' — here habitual or iterative past sense: 'would call/recite'. بهرام را is the proper name with the object marker; syntactically the spahbed 'recited/called [him] Bahram.'
segments.s666-g69: سپهبد بیامد is straightforward past 'the spahbed came.' بر + شهریار marks the direction/recipient 'to the king' (بر used here as 'to/toward' in classical usage). The clause is a movement verb with the destination following it.
segments.s666-g70: که introduces an indirect speech or purpose clause ('that' / 'in order that'); خوانم is first-person present/subjunctive of خواندن 'to read/recite' — here 'let me read' or 'I will read.' عرض را is the object: عرض in this context is a report/presentation (the roll or list) followed by the object marker را. ز بهر شمار is a purpose phrase 'for the counting' (ز بهر = for the sake of, شمار = counting/tally), explaining why he wants to read.
segments.s666-g71: ببینم is a jussive/subjunctive 'let me see' or 'I should see' (requestive or deliberative). ز لشکر 'from the army' gives the source, and که جنگی که‌اند is a relative clause 'which are warriors' (که = relative 'that', جنگی = 'warlike / warriors', که‌اند = 'that are' — the copula + plural clitic). The clause asks to examine the army's composition.
segments.s666-g72: گه here means 'sometimes' (an adverb); نام جستن 'seeking a name' = 'seeking fame' (نام = reputation, جستن = to seek). درنگی is a noun 'a pause/hesitation' so the phrase describes those 'who sometimes hesitate in seeking fame.' The final که‌اند again is the relative copula 'that are.'
segments.s666-g9: چنین modifies the verb داد and means “thus/so.” داد is the past verb “gave,” followed by its object پاسخ (“answer”); بدو is the dative “to him.” جنگجوی is the subject placed after the verb-object phrase (poetic inversion): “the warrior gave him an answer.”
segments.s666-g10: که introduces a subordinate content clause (“that …”). با + ساوه شاه = “with the king of Saveh.” آشتی نیست is the negation “there is no reconciliation/peace” (نیست = “is not”), and روی is a postposed noun meaning “appearance/face” used metaphorically (“no sign/appearance of peace”).
segments.s666-g11: گر is the conditional “if.” او is the subject “he.” جنگ را uses the object marker را to mark “the war.” خواهد + infinitive (آراستن) expresses intention/will (“wants/intends to prepare/equip” the war); here خواهد indicates his desire/intent.
segments.s666-g12: This clause has an inverted predication: آشتی خواستن (“to ask/request reconciliation”) is the verbal nominal phrase and هزیمت بود literally reads “would be rout/defeat.” بود is used here with a conditional/schematic sense (“would be/was”), so the line says that asking for peace would amount to a rout/defeat.
segments.s666-g13: و دیگر is a linking particle meaning “and additionally/again.” که introduces the clause. بدخواه “ill‑wisher” (i.e. enemy) is the subject; گردد is the classical third‑person form “becomes” (subjunctive/indicative nuance in poetry), and دلیر is the predicate adjective “brave/bold.”
segments.s666-g14: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” بیند is a present/optative third‑person form “he sees” (classical usage). که introduces the content of that seeing: کام تو آمد بزیر literally “your desire/will came down,” i.e. “your desire/fortune has been brought low.” آمد is past “came,” and بزیر is an adverbial “down/under.”
segments.s666-g15: گه is an adverb “sometimes/at times.” The clause رزم چون بزم پیش آوری is comparative/conditional: چون = “as/like” and آوری (classical present/subjunctive/second‑person form) means “you bring” or more generally “one brings/puts forward”; thus the phrase reads roughly “if/when you bring battle as/like a banquet (before others).”
segments.s666-g16: به + فرمانبری = “to/for obedience.” ماند here means “remains/stands” and the following این داوری (“this judgment/verdict”) is what remains: i.e. the decision/lesson is to remain obedient. The word order (prepositional phrase before predicate) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s666-g1: A nominal simile: شب تیره (literally 'dark night') is compared by چون ('like') to چادر مشک‌بوی. چادر here is a poetic 'veil/cover' (not 'tent') and مشک‌بوی is a postpositive adjective/compound 'musk‑scented' modifying it; word order (Noun + چون + simile) is typical in classical Persian imagery.
segments.s666-g2: بیفگند is a past (3rd sg.) poetic form meaning 'cast/throw (off)' used here to indicate the night being cast aside; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then'; خورشید is the subject of the following verb; بنمود is past tense of نمودن 'to show/appear' (here 'appeared/revealed'); روی is 'face' (object of 'showed').
segments.s666-g3: A short clause with a prepositional place phrase first (به درگاه 'to/into the court') followed by شد (past of شدن used here as a narrative verb 'came/arrived'); مرزبان is the logical subject appearing after the verb (classical Persian verb–subject order); نزد شاه is a locative 'in the presence of the king.'
segments.s666-g4: گرانمایگان (plural) is the subject 'the illustrious/noble ones'; برگشادند is past 3rd‑pl. 'they opened' (برگشودن) and precedes its object, here راه 'the way/road', which follows the verb as direct object—typical classical Persian ordering.
segments.s666-g5: جهاندار (the sovereign) is the subject; بهرام را uses the object marker را to mark Bahram as definite/direct object; پیش + خواند acts as a compound meaning 'called forward'—پیش functioning adverbially and خواند (past 3rd sg.) meaning 'called/summoned.'
segments.s666-g6: به تخت is a prepositional phrase 'onto/to the throne' (به = to/into); از بر نامداران is a classical locative phrase meaning 'in the presence of / beside the illustrious men' (از + بر + noun); نشاند is the past transitive verb 'seated (him)' (archaic past form used for 'set/sat down'). The verb closes the clause, with its complements preceding it.
segments.s666-g7: بپرسید is past narrative 'he asked'; زان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'); که introduces an indirect question/'whether'; با ساوه شاه is the prepositional phrase 'with the king of Saveh' (با + place/name + title).
segments.s666-g8: This string lists alternative actions in the embedded question. کنم is 1st‑person subjunctive/present used for 'should I do/make' (here 'should I make peace'); آشتی‌گر 'peacemaker' names one option; فرستم is 1st‑person subjunctive/present 'should I send/dispatch' (used for the alternative 'send a peacemaker' or 'send an army'); سپاه 'army' is the object of فرستم. Classical Persian often places multiple verb options in this compact way.
segments.s666-g73: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech). "سالار لشکر تویی" is the quoted/predicative clause: سالار لشکر = 'commander of the army' (noun + modifier, ezāfe omitted as common in poetry), and تویی is the 2nd-person singular copula 'thou art' (تویی = تو + ی). The whole segment reads literally 'to him said: you are commander of the army.'
segments.s666-g74: بتو = 'to you' (direction/beneficiary). باز گردَد is a 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative construction meaning 'may ... return' or 'may ... be returned' (باز 'back/again' + گردد 'it returns/come back'); the verb is in the classical/formal subjunctive used for wishes. بد و نیکویی are two nouns joined by و ('bad and goodness'), the order here is poetic; they are the items that 'return to you.'
segments.s666-g75: سپهبد بشد: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' and بشد is a past/inchoative verb ('he became/rose/went/arrived') used idiomatically here to mean 'the spahbed went/appeared.' تا in this position indicates direction/extent ('to' / 'as far as' the following place), and عرض‌گاه شاه = 'the king's audience-hall' (عرض‌گاه = place of audience). The sequence is poetic but means 'the spahbed went to the king's audience-place.'
segments.s666-g76: بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered (the king said)'. تا introduces the result/purpose ('so that/so that ...'), and the clause پیش او شد سپاه literally orders as preposition + pronoun + verb + subject: پیش او = 'in his presence/before him', شد = 'arrived / came to be', and سپاه = 'the army' (subject placed after the verb in poetic order). Together: 'he commanded, and the army came before him.'
segments.s666-g77: گزین کرد is a compound verbal idea: گزین (choose/select) plus the light verb کرد ('did'), yielding 'he selected.' ز ایرانیان is the source phrase 'from the Iranians' (ز = from, poetic for از), and لشکری here is the object 'a military force / a troop' (noun used as the thing selected). The word order places the source before the selected thing, a typical poetic flexibility.
segments.s666-g78: هرآنکس = 'whoever / any person who' and که is the relative/complementizer linking to the following clause. بود is the past copula 'was.' از سران افسری is a prepositional source/partitive phrase: از = 'from/among', سران = 'leaders/chiefs', and افسری qualifies them as 'officer(-class)' (افسری = officer-related). The whole relative clause modifies 'هرآنکس' — 'whoever was among the leaders/officers.'
segments.s666-g79: نبشتند is the past plural 'they wrote/inscribed.' نام is the direct object 'name(s),' and ده و دو هزار is the numeral expression 'ten and two thousand' = 'twelve thousand' (a poetic numeral order; 'ده و دو' = 10 + 2 then 'هزار' = thousand). The phrase thus means 'they wrote/inscribed the names of twelve thousand ...'.
segments.s666-g80: This is an appositive description of the twelve thousand: زره‌دار = 'armour‑bearers / armored warriors' (زره + دار), و = 'and', برگستوانور is a compound/obscure poetic term preserved in the manuscript (here left as a compound name of a troop-type) and is followed by سوار = 'riders/horsemen.' The segment lists troop types: 'armored and [برگستوانور] horsemen.'
segments.s666-g305: ز گفتار او is a prepositional/causal phrase ('because of his speech'); ز here = 'because of/from' and precedes the noun phrase. شاه خشنود گشت is a simple past-result clause: شاه (subject) + خشنود (predicate adjective) + گشت (past tense copular/verb 'became'). Note the older narrative past -َد ending on گشت gives a past result meaning 'became'.
segments.s666-g306: همه رنج پوینده is a noun phrase where همه ('all') quantifies رنج ('suffering/toil') and پوینده functions as a participial/attributive form meaning 'the one who seeks' or 'of the seeker' (so 'the seeker's toil'). بی‌سود گشت repeats the same copular pattern as above: بی‌سود (predicate adjective 'fruitless') + گشت (past 'became'), i.e. 'became fruitless.'
segments.s666-g307: سپهدار شبگیر is a compound title: سپهدار ('commander') qualified by the epithet شبگیر ('night-watching' or 'night-guard'). لشکر براند is verb-final: لشکر (object/thing moved = 'the army') + براند (classical narrative verb form with -د; here 'drove/led/put in motion'). The -د ending is the older past/narrative form.
segments.s666-g308: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'over/on them' (بر = 'upon/over'), and همی is the habitual/progressive adverb (classical 'kept/was') indicating ongoing action. نام یزدان is the direct object 'the name of God' and بخواند is another narrative past verb ('he recited/called'); here بخواند means 'recited/invoked.' Put together: 'over them he continually recited the name of God.'
segments.s666-g309: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/continuous past ('was going' / 'kept going') with the classical همی prefix marking ongoing action. تا کشور خوزیان uses تا in the sense of extent 'as far as/to' (here 'to the land of the Khuzis'); کشور خوزیان is the destination noun phrase.
segments.s666-g310: ز لشکر is a source phrase 'from the army' (ز = 'from'). کسی را is the indefinite object with را (object marker); in combination with the negated verb نیامد ('did not come'), کسی here effectively reads as 'no one' (classical Persian often expresses 'no one' by کسی + negative verb). زیان ('harm') is the noun that 'did not come'—literally 'no harm came to anyone from the army.' Note the object marker را appears before the negated verb as typical in older syntax.
segments.s666-g311: زنی با جوالی is a noun phrase 'a woman with a jo͞wāl(ī)' (with X). با = 'with' attaches an accompanying instrument/attendant. میان پر ز کاه is a sequence where میان here functions adverbially/'in the midst', پر = 'full (of)' and ز + کاه = 'of straw' (z used as 'of/from' in poetic prose). Together: 'a woman with a (small) sack, in the middle full of straw' → naturalized as 'a woman with a sack full of straw.'
segments.s666-g312: همی‌رفت is again the imperfective past 'was going/kept going.' پویان here is an adjectival/participle meaning 'moving/active' modifying the action (i.e. she was moving). میان سپاه places the action spatially 'among the army/troops' (میان = 'among/amongst', سپاه = 'army'). The whole clause: 'she was moving among the troops.'
segments.s666-g33: چه is the interrogative 'what'. گوید is a classical present/indicative verb 'says' (here used to introduce what the enemy will say). تو را uses the object/topic marker را after the pronoun to mark 'you' as the one being addressed; دشمن عیب‌جوی is the subject phrase 'the fault‑finding enemy' (adjective-like compound). Word order places the verb before the subject phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s666-g34: که introduces the reported clause 'that'. بی‌جنگ is an adverbial 'without battle/without fighting'. پیچی is a 2nd‑person singular present/optative form of پیچیدن here meaning 'you turn / you withdraw' (an older/poetic ending). ز is the preposition 'from', بدخواه 'ill‑wisher' and روی 'face' form the prepositional complement 'from the face of the ill‑wisher' (i.e. to turn away from the hostile face).
segments.s666-g35: چو here means 'when' (a temporal subordinate). بر دشمنان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against the enemies'. تیرباران is a verbal noun/adverbial meaning 'arrow‑showering' (i.e. raining arrows). کنیم is 1st‑person plural classical present/subjunctive 'we do / we make' (used for habitual or future action).
segments.s666-g36: کمان را uses را to mark 'the bow' as the direct object of کردن ('to make/turn [into]'). چو = 'like/as', ابر بهاران = 'cloud(s) of spring' (a simile), and کنيم repeats the 1st‑person plural verb 'we make'. The verb comes at the end as is normal in Persian; the construction means 'we make the bow like the spring clouds.'
segments.s666-g37: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' qualifying the following nouns. تیغ و گوپال are coordinated nouns 'the sword and Gopāl' (Gopāl here is a proper name). چون introduces a simile 'as/like' and صدهزار is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' used metaphorically to indicate great number or strength.
segments.s666-g38: شکسته is the past participle 'broken/shattered' used predicatively; شود is the auxiliary 'become/be' in 3rd‑person (here passive: 'will be broken' or 'are broken'). در صف کارزار is a locative phrase 'in the battle‑line/ ranks of battle' modifying where the breaking occurs. Together they express a passive result in the battle formation.
segments.s666-g39: چو = 'if/when'. پیروزی ما = 'our victory' (possessive order: noun + pronoun). نیاید is a classical negative present 'does not come/appear' (negative of آمدن). پدید is an adverb/verb 'to appear' used here with نیاید for emphasis, so the clause means 'if our victory does not come into view.'
segments.s666-g40: دل 'the heart' is the grammatical subject; از نیک‌بختی 'from good fortune' marks what the heart might be withdrawn from. نباید is the impersonal prohibition 'one must not / should not', and کشید is the infinitive/present of کشیدن 'to draw/pull' used in the idiom دل از ... کشیدن = 'to withdraw one's heart from' (i.e. to lose hope). The clause therefore forbids abandoning hope in good fortune.
segments.s666-g137: برین داستان is the classical contracted form of بر این داستان, literally 'on/for this tale'; نیز 'also' follows it; دارم is the present first‑person 'I have' (here best rendered 'I hold/call'); گوا is a noun 'witness' used predicatively, so the whole phrase means 'I also have/call this tale as witness' with the subject 'I' omitted as usual in Persian.
segments.s666-g138: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; بشنود is the third‑person present/subjunctive form of شنیدن here rendered 'should hear' or simply 'he hears'; شاه فرمانروا is a double title in apposition ('the king, the sovereign') and is the understood subject of بشنود (the object—'it' or 'the tale'—is omitted).
segments.s666-g139: که begins a relative/that‑clause ('that' or 'who'); کاوس کی here is the name with the honorific/title Ki (Kay) following the name (a formulaic epic form: 'Kay Kavus'); را is the definite object marker attaching to the name; بهاماوران is the agent 'the rumor‑mongers' (those who sow discord).
segments.s666-g140: ببستند is a past, third‑person plural verb 'they shut/they bound/they confined' (here 'they besieged/locked up'); با لشکری is an instrumental/with‑phrase 'with an army'; بی‌کران is an adjective 'boundless/endless' modifying لشکری, so 'with a boundless army.'
segments.s666-g141: گزین کرد is the compound verb گزین‌کردن 'to choose' presented here as past tense (literally 'chose'); word order places the verb before the subject Rستم (common in poetic/fixed phrasing): 'Rostam chose'; ده و دو هزار is the classical way to render 12,000 (literally 'ten and two thousand').
segments.s666-g142: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' (partitive); شایسته modifies مردان as 'worthy' or 'capable' men; گرد و سوار is a coordinated description attached to مردان meaning 'foot (troops) and horse (troops)' or 'on foot and mounted' — a conventional epic pairing describing both infantry and cavalry.
segments.s666-g143: بیاورد is a past tense third‑person singular 'he brought' (here 'he fetched/brought forth'); کاوس کی را is again the name plus title with را as the object marker; ز بند is the poetic preposition 'from (ز = از)' + بند 'fetter/bond', so 'brought Kay Kavus from bondage.'
segments.s666-g144: بران is an archaic/adverbial prepositional form meaning 'from that / on that account'; نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/illustrious (men)'; نیامد گزند is a fixed negative clause 'no harm came' (نیامد = 'did not come', گزند = 'harm'), so 'from that the renowned suffered no harm.'
segments.s666-g289: "بدو گفت" is literally 'to him he said' — بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him', followed by گفت 'said'. رو here is an imperative 'go' (archaic/poetic imperative of رفتن) and با سپهبد بگوی is an instruction 'tell the spahbed' (با + person marking the addressee; بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form 'say/tell'). Word order is terse/poetic: indirect object (بدو) + verb (گفت) + imperatives.
segments.s666-g290: که introduces the reported content ('that'). امشب = 'tonight'. ز جایی که هستی = 'from the place where you are' (ز + جایی = 'from a place'; the second که is the relative/connector 'that/where' linking to هستی 'you are' — here هستی is the 2nd-person present copula/verb). مپوی is a poetic negative imperative 'do not go/leave' (م- negative prefix + پوی 'go/seek'), so the clause commands not to move from the current place tonight.
segments.s666-g291: به شبگیر is an adverbial phrase 'at nightfall / by night' (به + شبگیر). برگرد and آی are two imperatives: برگرد 'return, turn back' and پیش من آی 'come before me / come to me' (پیش = 'in the presence of', من = 'me', آی = archaic imperative 'come'). The conjunction و links the two commands.
segments.s666-g292: This is a poetic/flexible word order for a future causative: تهی 'empty' is the adjective/result, کرد + خواهم together express 'I will make' (کرد 'make/do' with خواهم acting as future auxiliary 'I will'). ز بیگانه جای literally 'from strangers place' = 'the place free of strangers' — the preposition ز marks removal ('of/from'), so the whole clause means 'I will make the place devoid of strangers.'
segments.s666-g293: بگویم is a first-person present/subjunctive used here with a future/intentive force 'I will tell' (poetic register). بتو is the indirect object 'to you'. هرچ آید ز پند = 'whatever comes from counsel' (هرچ = 'whatever', آید = 'comes', ز پند = 'from advice'), forming the content the speaker intends to tell.
segments.s666-g294: سخن چند = 'a few words' (سخن 'speech/words', چند 'a few/several'). یاد آمدم is an idiom 'came to my mind / I remembered' (یاد 'memory/remembrance' + آمدم 'came (to me)'). سودمند 'useful' qualifies those words; overall: 'a few useful words occurred to me.'
segments.s666-g295: Simple declarative: فرستاده آمد = 'the messenger came' (subject + past verb). بر پهلوان uses the preposition بر 'to/toward' with پهلوان 'champion' to mark the direction/recipient of the arrival (to the champion). This is straightforward poetic word order.
segments.s666-g296: بگفت = 'he said / he told' (past reporting verb). آنچ بشنید مرد جوان is a relative clause 'that which the young man heard' (آنچ = 'that which'; بشنید = past 'heard'; مرد جوان = 'the young man', here the subject of بشنید placed after it, a common poetic order). So the messenger 'told that which the young man had heard.'
segments.s666-g129: به جای is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in place of / instead of.' جوانان شمشیرزن functions as 'the young swordsmen' (جوانان 'young men' + شمشیرزن 'swordsmen'); the plural noun جوانان precedes شمشیرزن here by poetic/colloquial word-order (appositive/attributive relation).
segments.s666-g130: چهل‌سالگان is a plural substantive 'men aged forty.' خواستی is a past-form verb meaning 'you asked/wanted' (second-person past in this address). ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and انجمن is the source 'assembly/meeting.' The phrase literally orders object then verb then source: 'forty-year-olds you asked from the assembly.'
segments.s666-g131: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / general.' چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' داد is the simple past verb 'gave' and takes پاسخ 'an answer/reply' as its object. بدوی is a poetic adjective 'original/prime/first' modifying پاسخ; the word order (subject — adverb — verb — object — adjective) is characteristic of epic diction.
segments.s666-g132: که‌ای here functions as the archaic/vocative particle (equivalent to که ای) introducing direct speech: 'O ...' شاه is 'king,' نیک‌اختر is an epithet/name attached to the king, و is the conjunction 'and,' and راست‌گوی is an epithet 'truthful, straight-talking' addressing the king. The phrase is a vocative formula.
segments.s666-g133: شنیدستی is the second-person past/perfect 'have you heard?' (a rhetorical or interrogative perfect used by the poet). آن داستان مهان = 'that tale of the guests' with آن as demonstrative before the noun داستان, and مهان ('guests/visitors') following as its qualifying noun.
segments.s666-g134: که introduces the relative clause 'which/that.' در پیش is a fixed locative phrase meaning 'in the presence (of).' بودند is the past plural copula 'they were,' and شاه جهان 'king of the world' is the subject/title; note the poetic inversion where the verb precedes the subject: 'were (they) the king of the world' → 'were before the king of the world.'
segments.s666-g135: که is a conjunction 'that/since.' چون here functions as 'when/since' (temporal/causal). بخت پیروز is a noun + adjective sequence 'victorious Fortune' (adjective following noun, typical in Persian), یاور is predicate noun 'helper/ally,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' Together: 'since/when Fortune was a victorious ally.'
segments.s666-g136: روا باشد is a conventional modal formula 'it is fitting / it would be proper' (باشد here functions as subjunctive/optative). ار = 'if.' یار 'companion/ally' and کمتر بود = 'were fewer' (کمتر 'fewer/less' with بود 'was/were' in the protasis). The whole conditional reads 'it is fitting if the companions were fewer.'
segments.s666-g113: همه precedes the noun phrase to mark totality ('all'); گنج‌های is the plural possessive/partitive 'treasures' (گنج + plural -ها with ezāfe-like linkage suppressed in poetry). سلیح نبرد is a noun chain where سلیح (an archaic/poetic form of سلاح, 'weapon/armament') and نبرد ('battle') together qualify گنج‌های — i.e. 'the treasures (and) armaments of battle' or more loosely 'the war-treasures/armories.'
segments.s666-g114: به ... به ... marks the locations (here 'into/in/at Pars and at Ahvaz'); پارس and اهواز are place names. در here is the noun 'door/gate' (not the preposition), and باز کرد is a compound verbal phrase 'open + do' (کرد as simple past of کردن) meaning 'opened' — i.e. 'opened the doors (of the treasuries) in Pars and Ahvaz.' Word order is typical poetic Persian with verb final (کرد) and its complements preceding it.
segments.s666-g115: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of.' اسبان جنگ is a genitive phrase 'the war-horses' (اسبان = horses, جنگ = war). آنچ is a relative/quantifying word 'that which / as many as,' بودش is the past copula with enclitic -ش ('it was / he had it'), and یله is an adjective 'loose, unbridled.' Together they mean roughly 'as many of the war-horses as were loose' or 'the war-horses that were unbridled.'
segments.s666-g116: بشهر is a poetic concatenation (به + شهر) meaning 'to/into the city.' اندر آورد is a verb phrase 'brought in' (اندر marks 'into/within' and آورد is past of آوردن 'to bring'). چندی گله is the object 'several herds' (چندی = some/several; گله here = 'herd, flock'). Standard Persian word order places the verb after its complements in poetic lines.
segments.s666-g117: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb used for 'he said/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('that/so that'). پهلوان سپاه is a title-like noun phrase 'the champion (پهلوان) of the army (سپاه),' with the modifier preceding the head in poetic diction.
segments.s666-g118: بخواهد is a subjunctive/optative form of خواستن used here after تا to express 'let him ask / should ask.' هرآنچش is the fused relative 'whatever / all that,' and بباید is the impersonal/modal older form meaning 'must/ought to be' (here: 'that he should be given' or simply 'that he should have'). ز + شاه marks the source 'from the king.' The clause as a whole gives the content of the command.
segments.s666-g119: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase 'thus said.' The object marker را attaches to بهرام, so بهرام را is the person addressed/affected (object/topic-marked), while شهریار (literally 'the king') is the subject that follows — a word order permitted in Persian poetry where the object (with را) precedes the subject for emphasis.
segments.s666-g120: که introduces a relative/content clause ('that' / 'who'). از هر دری is a prepositional phrase 'from every quarter/sea/side' (دری here used poetically to mean 'quarter/sea/region'), دیده is the past-participle/compound-perfect sense 'having seen' (i.e. 'he had seen'), and کارزار is the noun 'battle.' Together they mean 'who had seen battle from every quarter.'
segments.s666-g177: چو here is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. بشنید is past 3rd-person singular 'he heard'. گفتار او is the object phrase 'his speech' (noun + possessive pronoun), and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the object by poetic inversion; the clause reads literally 'when heard the speech of him the king'.
segments.s666-g178: چنان ... چون forms a simile/comparative: 'so/thus ... as'. تازه is a predicative adjective 'fresh/new', شد is the past 3rd-person singular 'became'. چون گل اندر بهار = 'as a flower in spring' where چون = 'as', اندر = poetic preposition 'in', and word order is the normal poetic string of comparison + noun + locative.
segments.s666-g179: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت used as past 'said'); what follows is an imperative command. رو here functions as the imperative 'go' or 'turn' in poetic diction, and جوشن کارزار is a compound object 'battle-armor' (جوشن = cuirass/armor, کارزار = battle).
segments.s666-g180: بپوش is an imperative 'put on' (second-person command). The conjunction و links the two imperatives; ز ایوان به میدان is a movement phrase 'from the iwan into the field' (ز = from, به = into/towards, میدان = field/arena). گذار here functions as an imperative 'pass/enter' (poetic usage).
segments.s666-g181: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). بیامد is past 3rd-person singular 'came'. ز نزدیک شاه literally 'from near the king' or more naturally 'near the king' — z + place phrase is a poetic locative/source construction; word order puts the place after the verb.
segments.s666-g182: کمر خواست is an elliptical poetic phrase: خواست is past 'he wanted/caused' and here functions idiomatically as 'he girded' or 'he put on his belt/loins'. The following nouns connected by و form a list of garments/armour items (خفتان, درع, کلاه) that he takes up or dons; the verbs are elided in the list.
segments.s666-g183: برافگند is past 'he threw' or 'he cast'. برگستوان is a (poetic/compounded) noun phrase serving as the object of the throwing; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and سمند is 'steed/warhorse' — the clause means he cast something over the horse. Poetic compounds and inversions make the exact object borderline, but the verbal-prepositional structure is clear.
segments.s666-g184: بر‌بست is past 'he fastened/closed' (بر‌بست = bound/secured). پیچان is an adjective/participial form 'twisting/coil-like' modifying کمند 'lasso/snare'. بفتراک is an adverbial/poetic particle indicating manner or instant (rendered here as 'with a twisting motion' or 'in a flash'); overall: 'he fastened the twisting lariat.'
segments.s666-g41: وزان پس is an emphatic temporal phrase: وزان (و + زان) = 'from that' or 'from then', with پس 'then/therefore' reinforcing the sequence. بفرمان is the prepositional/agentive phrase 'by the command/of the order (of)', دشمن is its governor 'the enemy', and شویم is the first-person-plural present-subjunctive/jussive of شدن used here to express future/expected result 'we shall be/come to be'.
segments.s666-g42: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that'); the coordinated adjectives بیهوش و بیجان و بیتن are predicative (each formed with the prefix بی- 'without'), and the final شویم repeats the verbal predicate agreeing with the plural subject — 'we become (unconscious, lifeless, bodiless)'. The sequence is poetic but grammatically a single result clause introduced by که.
segments.s666-g43: بکوشیم is the first-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative of کوشیدن used as an exhortation 'let us strive' or 'we should strive'. با introduces an instrumental phrase 'by/with', گردش آسمان is a genitive construction 'the turning/revolution of the heavens' (گردش = rotation, آسمان = heaven/sky).
segments.s666-g44: اگر is the conditional 'if'. در میانه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the middle/centre', and the following three words form a compact clause where آرد (brings) is the verb, سر ('head' = culmination/turn) is the thing brought, and زمان (Time) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order: 'if, in the middle, Time brings (it) to a head.'
segments.s666-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گفتار بهرام is a genitive: 'Bahram's speech/words'. بشنید is the simple past 'heard'. شاه is the subject; its placement after the verb is a poetic inversion: 'When the king heard Bahram's words...'.
segments.s666-g46: بخندید is the past tense 'laughed' (subject carried over from the previous clause). و simply coordinates the next predicate. رخشنده شد is 'became radiant/bright-faced' with رخشنده an adjectival predicate and شد the copular verb, and پیشگاه (here) denotes the royal presence/court that is described as becoming radiant.
segments.s666-g47: ز (poetic) = 'from'. پیش here means 'in the presence of / before', so ز پیش جهاندار = 'from before the sovereign' (i.e. out of his presence). بیرون شد(ند) is the verbal phrase 'went out / stepped outside', with شدند the past plural copula/auxiliary agreeing with the plural subject 'they' (implied).
segments.s666-g48: جهاندیدگان is the plural subject 'those experienced in the world / the veterans'; دل is the inner subject 'their hearts' and پر از خون is a predicative phrase 'full of blood' (پر used adjectivally with از marking 'of/from'), followed by شدند 'they became' — i.e. 'their hearts were filled with blood' (a poetic expression of agitation).
segments.s666-g297: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact past clause: چنین (“thus/so”) modifies داد پاسخ (“gave an answer”); داد is the past third‑person singular ‘gave’. The conjunction که introduces the reported content (a subordinate clause). لشکر ز راه is a prepositional phrase with ز (poetic از) meaning ‘from’ and راه ‘road/way’, so ‘the army from the road…’.
segments.s666-g298: نخوانند is third‑person plural present/subjunctive/negative of خواندن used here in the sense ‘call/recall’ (they should not call/recall). باز follows the verb as an adverb meaning ‘back/again’ (i.e. ‘recall’). ای خردمند شاه is a vocative address: ای + adjective + noun = “O wise king.”
segments.s666-g299: زره بازگشتن is a verbal noun phrase: زره ‘armor’ + the infinitive/masdar بازگشتن ‘returning’ (‘the returning of the armor’). بد آید expresses an unfavorable result with آید (3rd sg. ‘comes/happens’) and بد ‘bad/unfavorable’. بفال (poetic fused form of به فال) means ‘as an omen’.
segments.s666-g300: به نیرو is a dative/benefactive phrase ‘for/to the force (troops)’. شود is 3rd‑singular ‘becomes/is turned’. زین here is intended as ‘from/this’ (classical زین = از این), but the provided lemma senses are unrelated, so index -1 is used. سخن is ‘word/speech’, and بدسگال is an adjective ‘ill‑omened/ill‑fated’ that predicates the result.
segments.s666-g301: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when/if’. چو پیروز گردم = ‘when I become victorious’: پیروز is predicate adjective ‘victorious’, گردم (from گشتن) = ‘I become’ (1st sg.), and بیایم is the speaker’s verb ‘I will come’ (subjunctive/optative used for future). برت = بر + ت ‘upon/unto you’ (the enclitic ـت marking ‘your/you’ object of motion).
segments.s666-g302: درفشان کنم is ‘I will make (something) shining/standard‑bearing’: درفشان (adjective or noun ‘shining/standard‑bearer’) precedes the light verb کنَم (1st sg. present/future ‘I make/do’). لشکر و کشورت are the coordinated objects ‘the army and your country/realm’ (کشورت = کشور + enclitic ـت ‘your’).
segments.s666-g303: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause ‘the messenger came’. به نزدیک is a classical locative/approach phrase ‘to near/into the presence of’ and شاه is the object ‘the king’. Word order is Subject–Predicate–Prepositional phrase.
segments.s666-g304: بگفت is past ‘he said/told’. آنچه بشنید is a free relative ‘that which he heard’ (آنچه = ‘what/that which’; بشنید = past ‘he heard’). زان رزمخواه is an ablative phrase ‘from that warrior’ (زان = از آن, ‘from that’ + رزمخواه ‘war‑desirer/warrior’).
segments.s666-g17: بدو گفت هرمز که پس چیست رای — Word order is inverted: بدو ('to him') is an indirect object preceding the verb گفت ('said'); هرمز is the speaker. که introduces the quoted content/cognate clause 'پس چیست رای' = 'then what is (your) counsel'. پس is a temporal/contrastive 'then/so', چی(ست) is the interrogative 'what is', and رای is a noun 'opinion/plan' completing the question.
segments.s666-g18: درنگ آورم‌گر بجنبم ز جای — This is a two‑choice question compacted into one line. درنگ آورم‌گر is a poetic inversion of 'گر درنگ آورم' ('if I delay' / 'shall I delay?'), where آورم is the verb and گر is the conditional/optative 'if/shall'. بجنبم is a first‑person present subjunctive/imperative 'shall I move/hurry', and ز جای literally 'from (my) place'; the two options are linked by the contrastive sense of گر ... بجنبم.
segments.s666-g19: چنین داد پاسخ که‌گر بدسگال — چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave such an answer' (داد here is the verb 'gave'). که‌گر is a fused conditional 'که گر' = 'that if / should it be that', introducing a subordinate conditional; بدسگال is an evaluative adjective/noun 'ill‑fated' describing the person referred to.
segments.s666-g20: بپیچد سر از داد بهتر به فال — بپیچد is a present subjunctive/optative 'turns/should turn' (here 'turn away'), سر 'head' is the direct object of the verbal image 'turn the head away (from...)', از داد 'from justice' uses از to mark separation, and بهتر به فال is a short clause meaning 'better (to take) as an omen' — به فال is the prepositional phrase 'as an omen', so the whole clause says that such a turning away from justice is best read as an omen.
segments.s666-g21: چه گفت آن گرانمایه نیک‌رای — چه گفت is an interrogative formula 'what said' (i.e., 'what did ... say'), with گفت as the verb 'said' (here presented as a question about speech). آن is the demonstrative 'that', and the two epithets گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious' and نیک‌رای 'of good judgment' modify the person referred to.
segments.s666-g22: که بیداد را نیست با داد جای — که introduces reported speech 'that'. بیداد را (bīdād + object marker را) places 'injustice' as the topic/object; نیست is the negated copula 'is not / there is no', با داد جای literally 'place with justice' i.e. 'a place alongside justice' — so the clause asserts that injustice has no place where justice is present.
segments.s666-g23: تو با دشمن بدکنش رزم جوی — The line addresses 'you' (تو). با دشمن 'with/against the enemy' marks the adversary, بدکنش is a compound adjective 'bad‑acting / ill‑behaved' modifying دشمن, and رزم جوی is a compound meaning 'seek battle' (جوی here is the suffix/word for 'seeker'/'seeking'). The order is typical Persian: topic + modifiers + verbal noun.
segments.s666-g24: که با آتش آب اندر آری به جوی — که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. با آتش آب places the two elements 'with fire (and) water' together; اندر آری is an older poetic order meaning 'brings/puts into' (اندر = into/within, آری here functions as 'bring/places' in the poetic diction), and به جوی 'into the stream' is the destination. The clause describes an action of mixing/placing fire and water into the stream (a striking, paradoxical image).
segments.s666-g225: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('that/so that'), linking the main verb to the following action. با سپهبد is a prepositional phrase 'with the spahbed'; برفت is the poetic/past form meaning 'he went' (past of رفتن) completing the subordinate clause.
segments.s666-g226: This clause names the agent and his motion: سپهبد is the subject/title, سوی جنگ is the directional phrase 'toward the battle', and تازید is the past tense of تازیدن meaning 'he rushed/charged'. تفت appears as a stray or emphatic tail here and does not match the provided lexical senses.
segments.s666-g227: بشد is an impersonal/past occurrence verb meaning 'it happened / there came into being' (here introducing the event). لشکر is the subject 'an army/host', and از کشور طیسفون is the source phrase 'from the realm/country of Ctesiphon'.
segments.s666-g228: سپهدار بهرام is a nominal phrase (title + name) 'commander Bahram'. پیش functions as a preposition/adverb 'before, in front of', and اندرون is the noun 'inner quarters / inner court', so the whole phrase denotes Bahram positioned before the inner court.
segments.s666-g229: This is a noun followed by coordinated adjectives: سپاهی 'the host/army', خردمند 'wise', و linking adjectives, گرد is an epithet whose exact nuance here is dialectal/poetic (likely 'seasoned' or 'skilled') and does not match the supplied sense list, and دلیر 'brave'. Adjectives follow the noun and are linked by و.
segments.s666-g230: سپهدار بیدار is 'the commander, alert' (بیدار here 'alert/awake'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and نره‌شیر is the image 'male lion' — a direct comparison of the commander to a lion.
segments.s666-g231: به موبد is an indirect-object phrase 'to the mobed' (dative). چنین گفت هرمز is an inverted order meaning 'thus said Hormoz' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت is the verb 'said' though the supplied senses do not include the verbal sense), and که introduces the content clause that follows ('that ...'). Note the classical word order places the indirect object and the adverb before the verb and the speaker's name after it.
segments.s666-g232: دلیرست is a predicative adjective phrase 'is brave' (stative copular form), و links the next quality 'and', شادان 'joyful/joying'. به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'on/in the plain of battle' (دشت نبرد = battlefield), specifying where the man is brave and joyful.
segments.s666-g329: خروشی (a noun) is the subject (an indefinite clamor/cry). برآمد is a simple past / result verb (3rd sing.) 'arose/issued forth' following the subject, a normal poetic word order. ز + پرده سرای is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from (behind) the curtain of the hall'; پرده (curtain) followed by سرای forms a possessive/locative grouping (poetic omission of ezāfe is common).
segments.s666-g330: که‌ای here functions as the vocative particle (colloquially written as که ای) — a call 'O ...'. نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned/illustrious ones' and پاکیزه‌رای is an epithet/adjective after the noun ('pure-minded' / 'of clean heart'), an archaic compound with -رای 'minded/opinion'. Together they form the vocative phrase.
segments.s666-g331: هرآنکس که begins a relative/subordinating clause 'whoever that...'. او is the 3rd-person pronoun referring back to that person. برگ کاهی is a partitive/accusative phrase 'a straw (leaf of hay)' where برگ (leaf) combines with کاهی (straw) to specify the object. ز کس is a prepositional phrase 'from anyone'. This entire clause is the subject/topic of the following main clause.
segments.s666-g332: ستاند is the past (3rd sing.) form of ستاندن 'to take/tear/take away' (here 'takes/steals'). نباشدش is a negative impersonal/existential construction 'there will not be for him' (نباشد + enclitic -ش = 'for him'). فریادرس is a noun 'one who comes to the cry' = 'rescuer/succor'. Together: 'there will be no rescuer for him.'
segments.s666-g333: میانش = میان + enclitic -ش 'his middle / in his midst' (object/target). به خنجر is an instrumental phrase 'with a dagger' (به used as 'by/with'). کنمم (کنم) is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن often used to express intent or immediate action here 'I will do / I will make (stab)'. به دو نیم is an adverbial phrase 'into two halves' describing the result of the action.
segments.s666-g334: بخرید is an imperative/subjunctive form of خریدن 'buy' addressed to one or more persons (polite/poetic). چیزی is the indefinite object 'something'. که introduces a relative clause 'that'. باید is a modal 'must/ought', and بسیم is a subjunctive/optative form 'may suffice / be enough' (used predicatively: 'that will suffice'). The clause means 'buy something that will be enough.'
segments.s666-g335: همی‌بود is an archaic/habitual past form meaning 'would be' / 'was continually' (imperfective nuance). ز اندیشه هرمز here uses ز = 'because of / from' with اندیشه هرمز = 'the thought/mind of Hormoz' (the cause). به رنج is a predicative prepositional phrase 'in distress / afflicted', where به marks the state experienced.
segments.s666-g336: ازان is an archaic compound 'from that / of that' linking back to a previously-mentioned matter. لشکر is the noun 'army/host' followed by the place name ساوه (Saveh) specifying its origin. و ... و links coordinated items (here پیل 'elephants' and گنج 'treasure'), listing the components that caused the distress.
segments.s666-g81: چهل‌سالگان را نبشتند نام — The phrase puts the topical object first: 'چهل‌سالگان' (the forty‑year‑olds) is fronted and marked by را as a definite/topic marker. نبشتند is the past plural of نوشتن; here the initial ن is negative, so although the dictionary senses list gives 'they wrote', the form means 'they did not write/inscribe'. نام is the thing that would have been written (the 'name').
segments.s666-g82: درم بر کم و بیش از این شد حرام — This clause states a prohibition that came into effect. درم names the coin/amount; بر (here "over/than") links درم to the comparison 'کم و بیش' (an idiom meaning 'less and more' → 'less or more'). از این = 'than this / than that amount'. شد is 'became' and حرام is the resulting state 'forbidden'.
segments.s666-g83: سپهبد چو بهرام بهرام بود — چو here functions as a simile 'like/as'. The structure places the subject سپهبد, then the comparison چو بهرام, and the predicate بهرام بود (the tautological repetition 'بهرام بهرام بود' is a poetic intensifier: 'he truly was Bahram'). بود is the past 'was'.
segments.s666-g84: که در جنگ جستن ورا نام بود — که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'. در جنگ جستن = 'to seek (out) battle' where جستن is a noun/infinitive 'seeking' or 'to seek'. ورا here refers back (roughly 'for that / for it') and نام here means 'reputation' or 'renown'. بود is 'was' linking the clause: 'his renown was to seek battle.'
segments.s666-g85: یکی را کجا نام یل‌سینه بود — یکی را is topicalized ('as for one / a single man') with را used as a topic/emphasis marker. کجا is a rhetorical 'where?' (implying 'there was none'). نام یل‌سینه بود = 'the name/title "yil‑sineh" (lion‑chested) was' — i.e. 'no one bore the name "lion‑chested".'
segments.s666-g86: کجا سینه و دل پر از کینه بود — another rhetorical 'where?' continuing the negative sense. سینه و دل 'breast and heart' form a compound subject; پر از کینه = 'full of hatred/grudge'. بود is the past copula. The overall sense: there was nowhere a man called 'lion‑chested' whose breast and heart were full of hatred.
segments.s666-g87: سرنامداران جنگیش کرد — سرنامداران 'the leading/title‑bearing men' is the object; جنگیش (جنگ + یش) qualifies them as 'of the war' or 'for battle' (i.e. his war commanders). کرد is the past 'did/made/appointed' — 'he appointed them as the chief commanders of the war.'
segments.s666-g88: که پیش صف آید به‌روز نبرد — که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'. پیش صف 'before/the front of the line' (پیش = in front of), آید is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive 'may come' or 'come', and به‌روز نبرد = 'on the day of battle.' The clause states their intended role: to take the front rank in battle.
segments.s666-g185: The compound noun جهان‌جوی (subject) 'world-seeker' is followed by the preposition با 'with' that introduces a coordinated list of instruments/items (گوی و چوگان و تیر). The coordinating و links the three nouns; the whole prepositional phrase names what he carries/has with him.
segments.s666-g186: به میدان is a directional prepositional phrase 'into/to the field/arena' (به + میدان). خرامید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular intransitive verb meaning 'strode/pranced/strutted' and describes his entrance. خود is an emphatic pronoun 'himself' (emphasizing the subject) and با وزیر is an accompaniment phrase 'with the vizier' specifying who accompanied him.
segments.s666-g187: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed/commander'; بیامد is the past-tense verb 'came'. به میدان is the destination ('to/into the arena') and شاه immediately follows as a noun-modifier (noun–noun genitive) meaning 'the king's arena' (میدانِ شاه) — poetic word order places these phrases after the verb.
segments.s666-g188: بغلتید is past 3rd-person — literally 'embraced' or 'clasped'; in context it implies he embraced/covered (the king) and prostrated himself. در خاک is a locative phrase 'in/on the ground/in the dust' and پیش سپاه 'before the army' gives the setting; the direct object (the king) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s666-g189: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the 3rd-person object enclitic -ش 'him'. The following clause جهاندار کرد آفرین has جهاندار 'the sovereign' as subject and کرد آفرین literally 'made/sounded “afarin”' — i.e. uttered the exclamation 'bravo' or praised him.
segments.s666-g190: سپهبد is the subject; ببوسید is the past tense 'kissed'. روی زمین is a locative expression 'on the ground' (روی + noun) functioning as the verb's object/goal — Persian frequently puts such locatives after the verb.
segments.s666-g191: بیاورد is the past-tense verb 'brought'. پس 'then' is an adverb; note the poetic inversion: verb + adverb + subject (شهریار) + object (آن درفش). شهریار is the subject 'the monarch' and آن درفش 'that banner' is the object.
segments.s666-g192: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' linking back to درفش. بد پیکرش here is an archaic/poetic locative construction meaning 'on its body' (بد functions like an older form of بر/به; -ش is the 3rd-person possessive 'its' attached to پیکر). اژدهافش is a compound adjective 'dragon‑emblazoned/dragon‑formed' and بنفش is the color adjective 'purple' modifying the banner; adjectives follow the noun in this poetic line.
segments.s666-g153: از این مایه‌گر لشکر افزون بود is a causal-topic phrase: از (here 'because of / owing to') + این مایه‌گر ('this source/financier' — مایه‌گر is agentive, 'one who provides resources') introduces the reason, then لشکر افزون بود is the predicate ('the army was greater / large'). بود is the past copula. Word order places the causal prepositional phrase before the main clause, typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s666-g154: ز مردی و از رای بیرون بود is two coordinated phrases describing a lack or exclusion: ز مردی ('with respect to manliness' — ز here indicates the domain/area) and از رای بیرون بود literally 'he was outside of counsel' (بیرون = 'outside, away from'). The construction means he was deficient/removed in manliness and not within wise counsel; بود is the past copula linking these predicates to the subject implied in context.
segments.s666-g155: سپهبد که لشکر فزون از سه چار is a relative clause: سپهبد ('the spahbed/general') + که ('who/that') introduces the clause. لشکر فزون از سه چار = 'an army greater than three or four' — فزون ('greater/more abundant') takes the comparative marker از followed by چار ('four'); together it gives 'more than three or four'.
segments.s666-g156: به جنگ آورد پیچد از کارزار combines two verbal ideas. به جنگ آورد = 'brought (them/him) into war' (آورد is past 'brought/led'); پیچد is a poetic/epic-form verb meaning 'turns/engages' (here used in narrative sense, functioning like a past/inceptive) and از کارزار ('in/of the battle') locates the action. Together: 'he led them into war and turned/engaged in the battle.'
segments.s666-g157: دگر آنک گفتی چهل‌ساله مرد = 'the other (one) that you mentioned, a forty‑year‑old man.' دگر ('the other') + آنک گفتی ('that which you said/that you mentioned' — آنک is an archaic relative, گفتی functions as 'you said' in the phrase) and چهل‌ساله مرد is an apposition describing that person.
segments.s666-g158: ز برنا فزونتر نجوید نبرد: ز + [comparison] + فزونتر indicates 'than' (comparison with the young). نجوید is the classical negative-present form (ن‑ + جویَد) here meaning 'does not seek/desire'; نبرد = 'battle'. So the clause reads 'he does not seek battle more than a youth' — i.e., he is not more eager for fight than a young man.
segments.s666-g159: چهل‌ساله با آزمایش بود: 'the forty‑year‑old (man) was with trials' — با آزمایش ('with ordeal/test') describes that he had been tested; بود is the past copula. The phrase implies he was experienced or proven by trial.
segments.s666-g160: به مردانگی در فزایش بود uses two locative/qualitative markers: به مردانگی ('in/with regard to manliness/valor') and در فزایش بود ('was in increase' = 'was increasing/advancing'). This idiom means he was growing or improving in bravery; بود is the copula in past narrative.
segments.s666-g361: من (I) is the subject placed before the object-marked pronoun او را (him) — را marks او as the definite object. بدامت is an adverbial/archaic compound meaning ‘continually’ (literally ‘in duration’) and modifies the verb آورم. فراز (literally ‘height, up’ ) is the directional/locative complement: together فراز آورم = ‘I bring/raise (him) up high.’ آورم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form used here to express a future or volitional action (‘I will bring/raise’).
segments.s666-g362: سخنهای is the plural construct for ‘words/speeches’ and is the object of آورم. چرب is an adjective meaning ‘flattering’ (literally ‘oily’) and modifies سخنها; و links the two adjectives. دراز means ‘long’ (extended) and also modifies سخنها. آورم is repeated for stylistic/paratactic emphasis (same verb form as previous segment) meaning ‘I will produce/bring (such) speeches.’
segments.s666-g363: برآراست is a past/simple perfective verb meaning ‘he adorned/arrayed himself’ or ‘made himself ready.’ The following words name the person: خراد برزین — here برزین is a personal name; خراد is an epithet or adjective (‘wise’) attached before the name. به راه is a prepositional phrase ‘on/for the road’ marking direction or purpose (he prepared himself for the journey). Word order places the verb before the directional phrase for poetic compactness.
segments.s666-g364: بیامد is a past-tense verb ‘he came’ or ‘he set out.’ بران سو is a deictic/adverbial phrase meaning ‘to that side/thereupon’ (بران = ‘thereupon/that’ + سو = ‘side/toward’). که introduces a relative clause giving the content of the king’s instruction; فرمود is the past verb ‘(the) king commanded/said,’ with شاه as its subject (postposed for emphasis).
segments.s666-g365: چو is the conjunction ‘when’ introducing a temporal clause. بهرام را is the object (را marks Bahram as definite). دید is the past verb ‘saw.’ با او b-phrase (‘with him’) shows accompaniment and is followed by بگفت, the past-tense/simple perfective ‘he spoke/said to him.’ The clause thus means ‘When he saw Bahram, he spoke with him.’
segments.s666-g366: سخنها is the noun ‘the words/speeches’ (plural) and is the theme of the question. کجا is the interrogative ‘where.’ داشت is an auxiliary/past verb here meaning ‘had’ (possession or past progressive) combining with نهفت (past participle/adjective ‘hidden’) to yield ‘had hidden’ or ‘was hiding.’ اندر is an old preposition ‘in/within,’ so the full phrase reads ‘where had (he) hidden the words?’
segments.s666-g367: وزان is a compound of وَ + ز آن ‘and from that,’ linking back to the previous location. جایگه means ‘place/position.’ شد here is a past verb meaning ‘became/took place’ but functionally marks motion or change of state (‘moved/turned’) — in this context it indicates that the king set off. سوی ساوه is the directional phrase ‘toward Saveh,’ and شاه is the (emphatic) subject placed at the end for rhythmic/poetic effect.
segments.s666-g368: بجایی (classical/poetic usage) here functions as ‘to a place/spot’ (literally ‘to a place’). که introduces a relative clause describing that place. گنج و پیل و سپاه are a coordinated list ‘treasure and elephants and army’ describing what the place possessed. بد preceding گنج is an older/poetic modifier; in many Shahnameh passages such adjectives can have idiomatic force (intensifying or qualitative) rather than strictly ‘bad’ — the whole relative clause therefore means ‘a place where there was (notably) treasure, elephants, and troops.’
segments.s666-g353: چنان دان is an imperative instruction: چنان (so/thus) + دان (imperative of دانستن ‘consider/know’), giving the reader/listener a way to frame the matter. که introduces the content clause that follows. کنداورست is an archaic compound (کندآور + است) functioning as a predicate: ‘is a digger/trap-maker’ (present tense, descriptive).
segments.s666-g354: مپندار is a negative imperative (‘do not think / do not suppose’). کان is the archaic demonstrative/relativizer ‘that’ (pointing to the army), لشکری is the noun ‘army/troops’, and دیگرست is a present-tense predicate (‘is different / is another’). The whole clause forbids imagining the army to be otherwise.
segments.s666-g355: ازان راه نزدیک is a prepositional phrase (‘from that road, near/along that way’). The final two words, بهرام پوی, direct the messenger to go to Bahram: پوی here has an adverbial/imperative sense ‘swift(ly)/hasten’ modifying the motion toward Bahram (classical word order places the destination name before the motion-verb-form).
segments.s666-g356: This is an instruction: سخن هرچ بشنیدی is a subordinate clause ‘whatever speech/words you hear’ (هرچ = ‘whatever’, بشنیدی = past/perfect 'you heard' used to introduce the content), and آن را بگوی is the imperative ‘say/report that’ where آن (that) is marked by the object-marker را. (Note: بگوی is the imperative/form of ‘say’.)
segments.s666-g357: بگویش is the imperative ‘tell him’ (بگو + enclitic -یش). که introduces the content clause. من با نوید و خرام is the adverbial phrase ‘I, with نوید and خرام’ — نوید here means ‘promises/assurances’ and خرام is a noun meaning ‘a proud/prancing gait’ (together describing the manner in which the speaker will act).
segments.s666-g358: بگسترد is the verbal stem ‘spread/extend’ and is paired with the auxiliary خواهم following it (classical Persian permits the main verb before the future auxiliary), so بگسترد خواهم together yield the first-person future ‘I will spread/set out’. یکی خوب دام is the object phrase ‘a certain/good trap’ (یکی = ‘a certain/one’, خوب = ‘good/fine’, دام = ‘trap’).
segments.s666-g359: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning ‘must not / ought not’. که introduces the clause whose truth is proscribed. پیدا شود is a passive/impersonal verbal phrase ‘become found / be discovered’ (پیدا = found/discovered, شود = become/occur), and راز تو is the noun phrase ‘your secret’ (تو = your). The clause warns that the secret must not come to light.
segments.s666-g360: گر is the conditional 'if'. او بشنود is a potential/subjunctive clause ‘if he hears’ (بشنود is the subjunctive/potential form of ‘hear’). نام و آواز تو is a coordinated noun phrase ‘your name and آواز’; here نام = reputation/name, and آواز is used in the sense of ‘voice/fame’ (i.e. public renown) — together ‘name and fame’.
segments.s666-g121: شنیدی که opens a reported-question/clause: شنیدی (past of شنیدن) = 'have you heard' or 'did you hear' and که introduces the content of what was heard. با + نامور + ساوه + شاه is a prepositional/attributive group meaning 'the famed king of Saveh' — با here marks association/concern (classical usage equivalent to 'regarding' or 'in the case of') and نامور modifies ساوه شاه ('the famed Saveh king').
segments.s666-g122: چه مایه is an exclamatory quantifier 'what a quantity/what sort of' or 'how much of'. سلیحست is a contracted/poetic form (= سلاح است) meaning 'is armed/has arms' (the copula -است is fused). The following nouns گنج و سپاه are coordinated items that share the same predicate (the verb/copula is ellipted and understood for them): 'arms, and treasure, and army.'
segments.s666-g123: هم at the start means 'also' or 'even'. از marks cause/reason ('because of') and links to جنگ ترکان ('the war of/with the Turks'). او here functions as the possessor/agent tied to what follows (i.e. 'his') so روز کین = 'the day of vengeance'; the whole phrase means 'also, because of his war with the Turks, (on) the day of vengeance...'.
segments.s666-g124: به آوردگه = 'to/at the place of bringing' i.e. 'at the place of assembly/muster' (آوردگه is a venue noun). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and combines with بلرزد زمین (literally 'the earth trembles') — word order is inverted for poetic effect (normal prose: زمین بلرزد). بلرزد is third-person singular present/imperfect form 'trembles' (used here with a future/evocative sense).
segments.s666-g125: گزیدی is past 'you chose/selected'. ز لشکر is the classical poetic form of 'from the army' (ز = از). ده و دو هزار is a numeral phrase giving the number chosen; in epic/classical ordering this expresses the large number (conventionally understood here as 'twelve thousand' in context of such enumerations).
segments.s666-g126: زره‌دار and برگستوانور both modify the head noun سوار: 'armored and برگستوانور horsemen'. زره‌دار is 'armored (men)' or 'mail-clad warriors'; برگستوانور is an archaic/poetic compound name for a particular class of cavalrymen (the lexicon marks it obscure). سوار is the head noun 'rider/horseman'. The verb is omitted here (ellipsis) because it was stated earlier (گزیدی).
segments.s666-g127: بدین مایه = 'with this amount/with such resources' (بدین is a fused classical form meaning 'by/with this'). مردم به‌روز نبرد = 'the people on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'on the day', نبرد = 'battle'); this whole nominal phrase is the topic/ground for the following verb 'ندانم' ('I do not know').
segments.s666-g128: ندانم = 'I do not know' (negated present/past sense 'I don't know'). که introduces the subordinate clause. چون خیزد = 'how it will rise/arise' — چون = 'how' and خیزد (from خاستن) is used poetically for 'arise/begin/happen' in third person singular. این کار‌کرد = 'this affair / this action' (کارکرد = outcome/operation), the subject of 'خیزد'.
segments.s666-g217: یکی آرزو (a single wish) is the object phrase placed before the verb in poetic order. خواهم is the 1st-person present/future of خواستن meaning “I want / I will ask,” and از شهریار marks the source/recipient of the request (‘from the king’).
segments.s666-g218: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that’). با من ‘with me’ indicates accompaniment. فرستد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of فرستادن meaning ‘may he send / let him send’ and یکی استوار is the object phrase ‘one steadfast (man)’ placed after the verb in the classical/poetic order.
segments.s666-g219: که تا marks a purposive/result clause (‘that so that’). هر کسی کو is a relative expression ‘whoever/anyone who’ (کسی = anyone; کو is an archaic relative particle attached to کسی). نبرد آورد is a verb phrase with نبرد ‘battle’ as object and آورد ‘brings/causes’ (3rd-person) — i.e. ‘brings/engages in battle’.
segments.s666-g220: سر دشمنی is a genitival phrase meaning ‘the head of (an) enemy / of enmity’ in context (classical Persian often omits the linking particle). زیر ‘under / beneath’ is spatial, and گرد آورد is a compound/verbal sequence with گرد (around/about; forming element) + آورد ‘brings’ — together meaning ‘bring/turn under’ (i.e. place the enemy’s head beneath him).
segments.s666-g221: نویسد is 3rd-person present/past-poetic ‘he writes’ (classical style). بنامه (‘in a letter’/‘by letter’) is an adverbial phrase indicating manner. درون نام اوی literally ‘within his name’ (اوی = his) — here meaning he records/enters his name in the letter (or requests that his name be recorded).
segments.s666-g222: رونده is a participial/adjectival form ‘going forth / being spread’; شود is 3rd-person optative/passive ‘may it become / let it be’. در جهان is an adverbial phrase ‘in the world’, and کام اوی = ‘his desire / its fulfillment’ (کام = desire, اوی = his). Together the clause expresses a wish that his name be spread and his desire realized in the world.
segments.s666-g223: چنین گفت is a fixed poetic phrase ‘thus said’. هرمزد is the speaker (Ahura Mazda / Hormozd). که introduces the reported clause ‘that...’ and مهران دبیر is the subject + predicate with the copula omitted (classical Persian often drops است): ‘Mehrān (is) a scribe/secretary.’
segments.s666-g224: جوانست is an adjectival predicate ‘is young’ (جوان + است contracted to -ست). The following و coordinates two more predicate nouns/adjectives: گوینده ‘a speaker/eloquent one’ and یادگیر ‘a learner / one who learns’ — the copula is understood across the list, so: ‘(he) is young, (he is) a speaker, and (he is) a learner.’
segments.s666-g49: ببهرام is the prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to Bahram' (به + بهرام fused); گفتند is the past 3rd-plural verb 'they said' (here reported speech). کاندر سخن is a locative/temporal phrase 'in that speech' or 'in the course of the conversation' (کاندر = 'in which/wherein', سخن = 'speech'). The whole segment sets up reported advice directed to Bahram.
segments.s666-g50: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' (چو = 'when'); پرسد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'asks' (here used as general/habitual 'when he asks'); تو را is the object 'you' with the object-marking را; بس is an adverb 'enough/excessively' modifying دلیری; دلیری is the noun 'bravery/daring'; مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. Together: 'When he asks you in speech, do not (be) excessively brave.'
segments.s666-g51: سپاهست is a fused copular phrase 'it is an army / there is an army'; چندان is a degree adverb 'so / to such an extent'; ابا ساوه شاه is a following genitive group referring to the possessor (here 'of the king of Saveh') — the exact role of ابا is not clear from the senses given, but the phrase functions as the possessive 'the king of Saveh'. Grammatically this segment states the extent/size of the host.
segments.s666-g52: که introduces the result clause 'that/so that'; بر ... بر ... is a repetitive prepositional phrase 'against/on (even) the ant and the gnat' (بر = 'against/over' here); مور and پشه are small creatures used to stress minuteness; بستند is the past 3rd-plural 'they closed/blocked'; راه is the direct object 'the road/way'. The clause says the army blocked the road even to ants and gnats.
segments.s666-g53: چنان چون is a parallel pair 'just as / in the manner that'; تو گویی is the verb phrase 'you say' (گویی is an archaic/polite present/subjunctive form meaning 'you would say' or 'you say'), and همی is an archaic adverbial particle (here an intensifier/continuous marker 'indeed/just / continually'); پیش شاه is a locative 'in the presence of the king'. Word order places the manner ('چنان چون ...') before the statement about speaking to the king.
segments.s666-g54: که is the clause introducer 'that' (introducing the content of what is said); یارد is 'companion/helper' and here functions as the predicate of the reported clause; بدن literally 'body' modifies/qualifies یارد (a phrase like 'bodily companion' or 'companion in person'); پهلوان سپاه is a genitive grouping 'the champions of the army' (پهلوان governed by سپاه). Grammatically this gives the content of the speech: 'that you are the bodily companion of the host's champions.'
segments.s666-g55: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here preceded by such an adverb to mark reported speech); بهرام is the speaker's name and the grammatical subject; با مهتران is the prepositional phrase 'with/to the chiefs/elders' (با marking the audience). The segment introduces Bahram's address to the leaders.
segments.s666-g56: که‌ای here introduces direct speech (the particle ای used for vocative 'O' is attached to the clause); نامداران is the vocative 'men of renown' (addressed), و is the coordinating 'and'; کندآوران is another vocative plural describing a class of men addressed (the exact lexical nuance of کندآوران is obscure in the provided senses, but syntactically it is a second coordinated vocative). Together this is the opening of Bahram's direct address: 'O renowned men and ...'.
segments.s666-g241: بترسم is first‑person present/imperfect with the b‑prefix expressing a worry or potentiality ('I fear / I may fear'); که introduces a subordinate clause. او (subject) follows the conjunction, هم adds 'also', and به فرجام کار is a prepositional phrase 'at/to the outcome of the affair' (به + noun) giving the temporal/causal frame for the following verb.
segments.s666-g242: بپیچد is the third‑person singular present/subjunctive form with the b‑prefix used after expressions of fear/wish ('may he turn / might he turn'). The construction سر از ... بپیچد is idiomatic: سر (the thing turned—'head') plus از marks separation/'from', so here 'turn (one's) head away from' or 'forsake/turn against'. شاه is 'king' and پرودگار is a title 'the Lord/God' in apposition.
segments.s666-g243: همی is a classical iterative/continuative adverb meaning 'continually/keeps on'. در سخن is an adverbial phrase 'in speech / when speaking'. بس here functions as an intensifier modifying the noun دلیری ('great/ample bravery'). نمود is a past (3rd‑singular) verb 'showed / displayed'.
segments.s666-g244: به گفتار (به + گفتار) means 'in/by (his) speech' or 'in words'. با here marks the manner/instrument ('by/with'), governing شاه (the addressee) — 'in his speech to the king'. شیری is an adjective 'lion‑like / leonine' modifying the implied demeanor; نمود repeats 'showed / displayed' (past 3sg). Word order places the manner phrase before the final verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s666-g245: بدو is the indirect object 'to him'. The normal word order here is verb before subject: گفت (past 'said') followed by the subject هرمز ('Hormoz'); because the supplied sense list for گفت did not include a simple 'said', that sense is not matched (see senses). که introduces the reported content. در پای زهر is a temporal/locative phrase 'at the foot/brink/approach of poison' (پای used as 'edge/limit').
segments.s666-g246: میالای is a present/stative verb/adjective meaning 'is inclined toward' (literally 'leans toward'). It takes زهر‑ای ('a poisonous/poison‑like') as its object or complement; بداندیش modifies دهر, so the phrase ends with the subject دهر ('the age, the world, time') — a typical classical Persian order where verb precedes subject. The whole clause states that the age/world tends toward poisonous ill‑will.
segments.s666-g247: چون here introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. او is the subject 'he'; گشت (past of گشتن) here means 'becomes/turns' (i.e., 'becomes victorious'). پیروز is the predicate adjective 'victorious'; بر + place/target (ساوه) marks the object of victory 'over Saveh'. شاه at the end functions appositionally/predicatively ('as king' / 'to be king') pointing toward the office he might take.
segments.s666-g248: سزد‌گر is a conditional qualifying word meaning roughly 'if fitting / if he is deserving' (classical formation where -گر adds a concessive/conditional sense). سپارم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will entrust / I shall hand over'. بدو is the indirect object 'to him'. تاج و گاه ('crown and gāh') is a conventional pairing meaning 'crown and throne/seat/realm' — گاه here is poetic for seat/realm rather than literal 'time'.
segments.s666-g265: به پیش آمدش is a compact verb phrase: به + پیش (lit. 'toward/in the presence of') plus آمدش (آمد + -ش 'to him'), so literally 'came toward/in his presence'. سر فروشی is a nominal phrase 'selling/offering the head' used idiomatically as 'offering one’s head'; به راه is a locative 'on the road'. Word order is typical Persian: determiner/adjuncts before the verb phrase.
segments.s666-g266: ازو دور is an adverbial phrase 'at a distance from him' (ازو = 'from him', دور = 'distant'). بد پهلوان سپاه is a noun phrase in which بد qualifies پهلوان ('a fearsome/bad champion') and سپاه specifies his affiliation ('of the host/army'). The phrase states that this champion was remote from the addressee.
segments.s666-g267: یکی خوانچه introduces an indefinite subject 'a certain small box'. بر سر 'on (the) head' is the locative phrase; به پیوسته داشت is a verbal construction where پیوسته (connected/fastened) modifies داشت ('had' / 'kept'), so the whole means 'he had [it] attached to his head' or 'a box was fixed on his head.'
segments.s666-g268: بروبر is an adverbial/locational particle meaning roughly 'upon it / facing it' (an older contracted form); فراوان modifies the following noun as 'many' or 'plentifully'; سرشسته is a compound noun/adjective meaning 'composed/arranged (on the head)' and داشت is the past-tense 'had'. Together: 'upon it he had many arranged (things).'
segments.s666-g269: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed' — a high-ranking commander). برانگیخت is the past verb 'roused/spurred', taking اسب ('horse') as its object. از شگفت is an adverbial phrase 'out of astonishment' (از = 'out of/from', شگفت = 'wonder/astonishment'). The clause means the commander suddenly urged his horse, struck by amazement.
segments.s666-g270: بنوک here is a contracted/prepositional phrase (orig. به نوک) 'with the tip of'; سنان is 'spear'; زان سری is 'of/that head' (زان = 'from/that'), and برگرفت is the past verb 'took up / removed'. The sequence describes seizing or removing the head with the spear's point.
segments.s666-g271: همی‌راند is the habitual/imperfective past 'was riding on' (همی- indicating continuous/action in progress). تا introduces the limit 'until'; نیزه برداشت is a compound verb 'lifted/took up the spear' (برداشت = 'lifted/raised'), and راست qualifies how it was lifted ('straight/upright' or 'directly').
segments.s666-g272: بینداخت is the past 'threw/cast'; آنرا is the direct object pronoun 'it/that'; بران سو is a directional phrase 'to/onto that side' (بران = 'to/onto that', سو = 'side/toward'); که خواست is a relative clause 'which he wished' (که = 'which/that', خواست = 'wanted'). The clause thus: 'he cast it toward whatever side he desired.'
segments.s666-g321: بجستند is the past, 3rd-person-plural form of جستن here meaning 'they sought/found'. The phrase places the verb before its object (a common poetic word order). آن مرد را is the definite object ('that man' + object marker را). در زمان is an adverbial phrase 'in time / immediately' (here 'at once') modifying the verb.
segments.s666-g322: کشیدند is past 3rd-plural of کشیدن used here in the physical sense 'they dragged/brought'. نزد + noun means 'into the presence of / before'. سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a military commander); دمان here is a proper name and functions as the name of the spahbed.
segments.s666-g323: The sequence X را گفت Y (ستاننده را گفت بهرام گرد) is the familiar Old/poetic order meaning 'Y said to X'. Here گفت is the past 'said' and بهرام گرد is the speaker (Bahram + the epithet/part of the name). ستاننده را is the person addressed ('to the collector') marked by را.
segments.s666-g324: گناهی که کردی is a noun + relative clause 'the sin that you committed' (کردی = past 2nd-person 'you did'). سرت را is the direct object 'your head' (سر + pronominal suffix) with را. ببرد is a 3rd-person form of بردن used here with the sense 'to take/carry off' (i.e. 'they will take your head' / 'they will cut off your head'); the verb follows its object in this poetic syntax.
