segments.s666-g325: دوانش is دوان + enclitic ش (him) meaning 'they drove/hurried him' or 'they ran him' (دوان here denotes causing motion). به پیش سراپرده is the destination 'before/in front of the inner chamber/behind the curtain' (پیش = in the presence of). برد is the main past verb 'brought'/'led' (verb sense not supplied in the given sense list).
segments.s666-g326: A series of body-parts linked by و ('and'): سر و دست و پای + ش (the pronominal suffix 'his') = 'his head, hands and feet'. شکستند is past 3rd-plural 'they broke' (here 'broke/fractured'), and خرد functions as a result/complement 'into small pieces' (خرد = 'small' / 'to pieces').
segments.s666-g327: میانش = میان + enclitic ش ('his/it') meaning 'through his middle / in the middle of him'. به خنجر is the instrumental phrase 'with a dagger' (به used instrumentally). به دو نیم کرد literally 'made into two halves' (به + دو نیم = 'into two halves'; کرد = 'made/did', here 'cut him into two').
segments.s666-g328: بدو here is the dative/locative 'to him/unto it' ('unto him'); مرد بیداد is a nominal phrase 'the man of wrongdoing/the unjust man' (مرد + بیداد as epithet). را marks the direct object of بیم کردن ('to cause fear'); بیم is 'fear' and کرد is the past 'he/they caused' — 'it caused fear to that unjust man' (i.e. 'it filled that wicked man with fear').
segments.s666-g369: ورا is the enclitic/object pronoun 'him' placed before the verb for emphasis (object + verb order). دید is simple past 'saw'. بستود is past tense 'he praised.' بردش is the verb برد 'took/received' with the clitic -ش 'him/his' attached, and نماز is the thing taken/accepted (here 'salute/obeisance' or 'prayer'); the verb-plus-clitic + noun order is a poetic inversion compared with ordinary modern Persian.
segments.s666-g370: شنیده is a past participle ('having heard' / 'on hearing'), used adverbially to set the circumstance. همی‌گفت is the habitual/progressive imperfective 'kept saying' (poetic auxiliary + present stem). با او 'with him' is a simple prepositional phrase, and به راز 'in secret' is another prepositional phrase (به + noun) showing manner/privateness.
segments.s666-g371: بیفزود is the simple past 'he added' (bi‑prefix is common in classical Persian verbs). پیغامش is 'his message' (پیغام + possessive clitic -ش). از هر دری is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'from every quarter / by every avenue' (here: sending the message by all means).
segments.s666-g372: بدان here functions as a deictic/purposive 'to that (end)' (classical be + آن → بدان) introducing the result/purpose. تا ... شود is a purpose/result clause 'so that ... may become / may be,' with شود as the potential/auxiliary 'become/be' and لشکر اندر هری 'an army in Harī' the clause's content (اندر = in/within).
segments.s666-g373: چوآمد is a subordinating past form meaning 'when he came' or 'as soon as he came.' It introduces a temporal clause; به دشت هری نامدار is the goal/place phrase 'to the famed plain of Harī,' with the adjective نامدار following the noun in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s666-g374: سراپرده زد uses زد (literally 'struck') in a poetic sense 'pitched/erected' and سراپرده 'the chief/inner tent' as its object (verb + object). بر لب جویبار is a locative phrase 'on the bank (brink) of the stream' where لب means 'brink/edge.'
segments.s666-g375: طلایه is 'vanguard/forefront.' بیامد is past 'came.' ز لشکر is the ablative/genitive 'from the army' (ز is poetic for 'from'), and به راه 'on the road' indicates the route or position. Word order is typical of epic diction, compact and slightly inverted.
segments.s666-g376: بدیدند is past 'they saw.' بهرام را uses را as the definite-object marker (here marking Bahram as the object). با سپاه is an accompaniment phrase 'with an army/host.' Note the simple past verb precedes the object phrase, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s666-g377: طلایه (vanguard) is the noun that begins the clause; بدید is the simple past, 3rd person singular 'saw'. The phrase آن دلاور سپاه is the direct object 'that brave (one) of the host' — آن is a demonstrative, دلاور a noun 'brave/hero' qualifying or appositive to سپاه ('the host/army'). The clause therefore reads literally 'the vanguard saw that hero of the army.'
segments.s666-g378: بیامد is the past tense 'came'; دوان is an adverbial/participial element 'running, rushing' giving manner ('came running'). تا here has the sense 'as far as / to' (marking destination), and بر is the dative/toward preposition 'to' used with the place-name ساوه (Saveh). شاه 'king' names the person he ran to. Word order places the manner (دوان) immediately after the verb, and the destination introduced by تا + بر.
segments.s666-g379: بگفت is the reporting verb 'he said'. آنک functions as a conjunction/relative 'that' introducing reported content. با + نامور مهتری is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with/by a renowned chief' — با 'with', نامور 'renowned', and مهتری here means 'chief/leader' (an epithet of the commander). This whole prepositional phrase modifies the ensuing verb 'came'.
segments.s666-g380: یکی لشکر آمد is a nominal subject phrase plus past verb: یکی (indefinite 'a certain' / 'one') modifies لشکر 'host/army', and آمد is past 'came'. به دشت هری is a locative phrase 'into/onto the plain of Hari' (به used as 'into/onto'). The clause is straightforward: 'a certain host came into the plain of Hari.'
segments.s666-g381: سخنها 'the news/words' is the topic. چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). زو is the contracted poetic form of از او ('from him'/'of it') — here marking source (i.e., 'when he heard of it'). ساوه شاه is the title 'the king of Saveh'; note the slightly archaic order where the title follows the clause. Overall: 'When the king of Saveh heard the news...' (زو here is an enclitic contraction; orthography in the line joins preposition + pronoun).
segments.s666-g382: پر اندیشه شد is a predicative phrase 'became full of thought' — پر 'full (of)' precedes the abstract noun اندیشه 'thought' and شد is the past of شدن 'became'. مرد جوینده راه is an appositive/epithet: مرد 'man' (here general 'man/person'), جوینده 'seeking' as a participial adjective 'seeker', and راه 'way/path' — together 'a man seeking the way', describing the king's state (pensive and searching for a course of action).
segments.s666-g383: ز خیمه is a prepositional phrase 'from the tent' (ز = poetic 'from', خیمه = 'tent'). فرستاده را uses فرستاده 'messenger' plus the object marker را indicating that he is the direct object. باز خواند is a verb phrase 'called back' — باز here is the adverb 'back/again' and خواند (from خواندن) in this context means 'to call/summon' (i.e., he called the messenger back). Word order places the origin phrase before the verb, and را marks the returned messenger as the object.
segments.s666-g384: به تندی فراوان is an adverbial ensemble 'with great speed / very hastily' (به + noun used adverbially, تندی 'haste', فراوان 'very/much'). سخنها is the object 'words/news', and براند is a past tense (poetic) form of راندن here used metaphorically 'to drive forth / to utter' — together meaning 'he uttered many words with great haste.' The clause uses two past verbs (خواند … براند) describing successive actions.
segments.s666-g161: بیاد آیدش is a compact expression: بیاد (به یاد) + آید + -ش (3rd-person clitic). Literally “it comes to his memory” = “he remembers.” مهر نان و نمک is the remembered thing: مهر (here = kindness/favour) modifying the compound نان و نمک (bread-and-salt, a set phrase for hospitality). Word order places the verbal phrase before the object for poetic effect.
segments.s666-g162: This clause is inverted poetically. گشته باشد is perfect participle (گشته) + auxiliary باشد giving a sense like “has become / has turned (may have become).” فراوان is an adverb/modifier “greatly/abundantly.” فلک is the subject (here meaning fate/destiny). برو is an archaic/poetic particle here functioning in the clause (lexically related to ‘go/turn’), so the sequence reads roughly “(therefore) the heavens/fate have turned greatly.”
segments.s666-g163: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’ (poetic variant of از). گفتار بدگوی is a noun phrase: گفتار (speech/words) modified by بدگوی (ill-speaking, i.e. slanderous). وز is the sequence ו + ز (and/from) linking the following compounds; نام و ننگ is a coordinated pair meaning ‘name (reputation) and shame.’ Together: “from the words of slanderers and from (fear of) reputation and shame.”
segments.s666-g164: هراسان بود = adjective (هراسان, frightened) + past copula بود (‘was’): “he was frightened.” سر نپیچد is negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن with سر (head) as direct object: نپیچد = “does not turn/does not turn away.” In classical usage a present/subjunctive form is used in a past narrative after بود to describe habitual or characteristic behaviour: here, “he would not turn his head away from battle” (i.e. he did not shirk fighting). ز جنگ = “from battle/war.”
segments.s666-g165: ز بهر is a poetic equivalent of برای (for/for the sake of); بهر is the headword meaning ‘for/toward.’ The coordinated list زن و زاده و دوده gives the things reckoned as motives: wife (زن), offspring (زاده), and دوده (literally ‘smoke’ — often used metonymically for household fire or livelihood). را at the end marks the whole phrase as definite/accusative (object/topic emphasis).
segments.s666-g166: بپیچد is the present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن with a prefixed بـ (poetic/fluent form) and here conveys ‘twists/wraps/wrends’ (i.e. exerts/twists). روان = soul/spirit; مرد فرسوده = ‘the worn-out/exhausted man’ (noun + adjective). The trailing را is the object marker: the verb governs the object روانِ مردِ فرسوده را. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis — verb before its object — a common poetic device.
segments.s666-g167: This is a general conditional/habitual statement: جوان (a young man) + چیز بیند (if/when he sees something). بیند is the present/subjunctive used for conditional/habitual (‘if/when he sees’). پذیرد is ‘he accepts’ (present), and فریب is the object ‘deception/trick.’ The clause states a general tendency: a young man who sees something will accept the lure.
segments.s666-g168: بگاه = ‘at the moment / at that time.’ درنگش = درنگ (pause/hesitation) + -ش (his), i.e. ‘his hesitation.’ نباشد is the negative of بودن (here ‘there is not / he does not have’), and شکیب is a noun meaning ‘patience’ (or archaic adjective ‘patient’). Put together: ‘at that moment his hesitation/pausing is not; he has no patience/hesitation.’
segments.s666-g145: همان نیز is an emphatic adverbial phrase: همان 'that/the same' (pointing back to someone already mentioned) + نیز 'also/likewise', so together 'likewise/that same, too'. گودرز is the proper name that follows; کشوادگان is an epithet placed after the name (poetic word order) and functions as a postpositive descriptive noun/compound attached to Goudarz. The whole segment is a nominal phrase with the name and its epithet introduced by a emphasizing adverb.
segments.s666-g146: سر here means 'chief/leader' (a title) and heads a noun phrase that is modified by the following nouns. نامداران and آزادگان are descriptive nouns in sequence (poetic apposition rather than a strict ezāfe construction) specifying what kind of people he is chief of: the renowned (نامداران) among the free/freemen (آزادگان).
segments.s666-g147: به here is the preposition 'for/to' marking purpose or object ('for the vengeance of...'). کین 'vengeance/grudge' is the noun governed by به, and سیاوش is the name in genitive relation ('the vengeance of Siyâvash'). ده و دو هزار is a classical additive numeral phrase ('ten and two thousand'), i.e. twelve thousand; Persian poetic lines often list the parts additively rather than using a single compounded numeral.
segments.s666-g148: بیاورد is the past narrative verb 'he brought' (3rd person singular). What follows is the object: برگستوانور is an archaic/obscure compound epithet modifying سوار (riders/horsemen); poetic word order places the descriptive compound before the head noun. The verb-before-object order (بیاورد ... سوار) is normal in narrative Persian poetry.
segments.s666-g149: همان نیز again is 'likewise/that same, too'. پرمایه is an adjective meaning 'rich/substantial' and stands before the proper name اسفندیار as an epithet (poetic ordering). The whole segment is a nominal phrase identifying Esfandiyar with the epithet 'پرمایه'.
segments.s666-g150: بیاورد is the past-tense narrative 'he brought'. جنگی here is used substantively/adjectivally to mean 'warlike ones/warriors' or 'a warband' and is the object quantified by the numeral phrase that follows; the segmented word و (and) is the connective inside the numeral 'ده و دو هزار' (ten and two thousand = twelve thousand). The verb precedes its object as is common in poetic narrative.
segments.s666-g151: به + ارجاسب marks direction/recipient: 'to Arjasb'. بر here governs چاره ('on/for a remedy/plan') so بر چاره کرد literally 'devised/put into effect a remedy/plan'. آنچه کرد is a relative clause 'that which he did/whatever he did' (آنچه 'that which' + کرد 'he did'). The sequence reflects poetic compacting of a clause meaning 'he turned to Arjasb for counsel and did what he did.'
segments.s666-g152: از آن is 'from that' (source or those things). لشکر و دژ are coordinated nouns 'army and fortress'. برآورد is used as a verbal element meaning 'brought about/raised/caused' (here taking لشکر و دژ as its objects), and گرد functions as an adverbial/locative particle 'around' (giving the sense 'raised an army and set a fortress around [it]'). The clause thus means he mustered an army and set up a fortress from that source.
segments.s666-g385: بدو گفت = 'to him (بدو) he said (گفت)'; classical word order often places the indirect object/proclitic before the verb, so بدو precedes گفت. کای is the vocative marker 'O' addressing someone, here followed by the name ریمن. پر فریب is an adjectival phrase: پر = 'full (of)' + فریب = 'deceit/trick' (postnominal adjectival phrase describing ریمن).
segments.s666-g386: مگر introduces a rhetorical question/contrast here ('surely not?/have you not?'). کز is the contracted classical preposition phrase که + از = 'from which / from', linking فرازی (a high place) to نشیب (a descent). ندیدی is the simple past (second person singular) with negative prefix ن-: 'you did not see / haven't you seen' — placed at the end as the matrix verb; نشیب is the noun 'descent' and is the object of دیدن. Word order is typical older Persian: (question particle) + (prepositional phrase) + verb.
segments.s666-g387: برفتی is an archaic/jussive-looking form used here as an instruction 'go away / depart' even though morphologically it resembles a past form; classical Persian sometimes uses such forms for hortative/jussive sense. ز (archaic form of از) marks origin 'from', and درگاه آن خوارشاه = 'the court/threshold of that base/vile king' (درگاه = court/presence; آن = 'that'; خوارشاه = epithet 'base king').
segments.s666-g388: بدان here functions as 'to/there (به + آن)' — 'go there'; تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'. مرا is the object 'me' marked by را (the object/definiteness marker). دام سازی is a purpose clause: دام = 'trap' and سازی here is a jussive/present-subjunctive 'you make / you set' (after تا, expressing purpose: 'that you may set a trap'). به راه = 'on the road' (به + راه, locative/directional). Word order: purpose clause follows تا and ends with the locative phrase.
segments.s666-g389: به جنگ آوری is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring to war / lead into battle': به + جنگ (to war) + آوری (from آوردن 'to bring' — here second-person or infinitival/jussive sense). پارسی لشکری is a noun phrase 'Persian troops/army' with the adjective پارسی before the noun لشکری. Classical Persian often omits auxiliary markers for tense; آوری can function as a prediction/imperative depending on context.
segments.s666-g390: زنی خیمه is a nominal clause 'a woman [a] tent' with an implied verb such as 'pitched' or 'set up' understood from context (classical Persian frequently omits a light verb). در مرغزار هری is a locative phrase: در = 'in', مرغزار = 'meadow', هری = the Hari (river/region). Word order places the locative after the nominal phrase.
segments.s666-g391: چنین گفت is a stock phrase 'thus said'; گفت is the verb 'said' (here following the adverb). خراد برزین is the speaker's name/title (here treated as 'Barzin the Wise'), and به شاه marks the indirect object 'to the king' using the preposition به. Word order = adverb + verb + speaker + indirect object.
segments.s666-g392: که introduces the reported content clause 'that ...'. پیش here means 'in front of / before / facing' and forms a prepositional phrase with سپاه تو ('your army'). اندک سپاه is a predicative nominal 'a small host' (اندک = 'few/small' modifying سپاه). Classical Persian commonly omits the copula 'است', so the clause reads literally 'that before your army (is) a small army'.
segments.s666-g25: وگر is a conditional 'if'; خود here is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun 'itself'; دگرگونه is an adjective 'different/other'; باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here as 'be' in a conditional clause; سخن is the noun 'speech/matter' and is placed at the end as the topical subject of the clause (poetic SOV order).
segments.s666-g26: This line uses inverted poetic order: گزیند (chooses, 3rd sg.) is the verb, شهی نو ('a new royal one' = 'a new sovereign') is its object, and سپهر کهن ('the ancient sky/heavens') is the agent/subject placed after the verb. سپهر is an elevated/poetic word for 'heaven(s)'.
segments.s666-g27: چو here is temporal/conditional 'when'; نیرو is the direct object 'strength/force'; ببازوی is a fused prepositional expression meaning 'on/into the arm' (ب + بازوی) and takes the following possessive خویش + ش = 'his own'; آوریم is 1st-person plural 'we bring/put' (present/subjunctive in poetic register). The overall sense: 'when we put strength into his arm'.
segments.s666-g28: هنر 'skill/virtue' is the thing being presented; هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever'; داریم is 'we have' (present); پیش آوریم is a compound verb phrase 'we bring forward' or 'we present' (پیش as adverbial 'forward/before' + آوریم 'we bring'). Word order is typical poetic: object/topic (هنر) comes before the clause that qualifies it.
segments.s666-g29: نه ... بود is a negative existential construction 'there was not'; از marks the agent/source 'from/by'; پاک‌یزدان is an epithet meaning 'the pious' or 'god-fearing ones'; نکوهش is 'reproach/censure'; بود is past copula 'was'. Literally: 'There was no reproach from the pious.'
segments.s666-g30: Again a negative clause: نه شرم 'no shame'; از یلان 'from the heroes' (i.e. the heroes felt no shame); چون here connects causally/temporally ('when' / 'since') to the following noun; پژوهش 'inquiry, scrutiny, or trial' is the reason or circumstance; بود is 'was'. The whole: 'Nor was there shame among the heroes, since/when there was scrutiny.'
segments.s666-g31: چو is conditional 'if'; ناکشته is a past participle 'not killed' (negated participle modifying the implied men); ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the group 'from/of the Iranians'; ایرانیان is the noun 'Iranians' and the numeral 'ده هزار' (not listed among the given tokens) attaches to indicate quantity. The phrase means 'if ten thousand of the Iranians are not killed'.
segments.s666-g32: بتابیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive of تافتن/تابیدن here with the sense 'endure, hold out, persevere'; خیره‌سر is a predicative/adjectival complement 'headstrong/obstinate'; از کارزار uses از + کارزار to indicate relation to the battle ('in/through the fight' or 'during the campaign'). The clause means 'we will endure, headstrong, in the battle.'
segments.s666-g169: ندارد is third-person singular present negative (نـ + دارد) meaning 'does not have' and here governs a following list of nouns joined by و. The sequence زن و کودک و کشت و ورز is a coordinated list of possessions (wife, child, cultivation/agriculture), with کشت و ورز functioning as a compound pair 'crops/tilled land and husbandry'.
segments.s666-g170: بچیزی ندارد is a fixed expression 'has nothing' (ب + چیزی → without anything; ندارد = 'does not have'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and ز ناارز ارز ('of worthless value') is a genitive/qualifying phrase: ناارز modifies ارز to mean 'of no/low worth.'
segments.s666-g171: چو introduces a conditional 'if/when'. بی‌آزمایش is an adjective 'untried/inexperienced' modifying the implied subject. نیابد (from یابیدن) is third-person singular meaning 'does not/does not come to find' or 'fails to attain' and takes خرد ('wisdom') as its object; classical Persian frequently places the verb before the object.
segments.s666-g172: سر مایه is a compound meaning 'chief resource/capital' (سر = head/chief + مایه = resource). کارها ('affairs') specifies whose capital it is. ننگرد (classical form of نگردد/نگردد) is third-person singular meaning 'does not regard/consider' or 'will not take care of', with the implied subject carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s666-g173: گر is a conditional 'if'. ایدون (an archaic/classical form of 'to come to pass') + که introduces a content clause ('that it comes to pass that ...'). پیروز گردد combines an adjective 'victorious' with گردد (subjunctive/optative/classical 'become'/'be') and به جنگ ('into/at battle') as the circumstance.
segments.s666-g174: شود is third-person singular 'becomes' or 'it happens that'. شاد و خندان are adjectival predicates 'joyful and smiling' joined by و. سازد درنگ uses ساختن ('to make/do') with the noun درنگ ('a pause') so 'he makes/takes a pause' — a stylistic way to say 'he pauses'.
segments.s666-g175: وگر is an alternative conditional form ('and if' / 'but if'). هیچ here functions as an intensifier 'ever/at all' (classical usage). پیروز شد is a perfective/past-form sequence 'became victorious' (classical narrative), and بر تنش ('on his body'/ 'on his person') is a prepositional phrase with the possessive clitic -ش attached to تن.
segments.s666-g176: نبیند (نـ + بیند) is negative third-person singular 'he does not see' / 'he finds not'. جز = 'except'. از پشت او is a locative phrase 'from/at his back' and دشمنش ('his enemy') is the exception object; the clause thus says he sees no one but his enemy at his back.
segments.s666-g345: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic -ش (his/its) = “his army.” نگه کن is an imperative phrase: نگه (a noun/gerund 'watch') + کن (imperative 'do') = 'keep watch' or 'look.' که introduces an indirect interrogative/purpose clause ('to see/that'), and چند و چیند is an elliptical classical phrasing meaning 'how many (چند) and how arranged (چیند)' — چیند is an older/poetic third‑person plural/participial or stative meaning 'are arranged/arrayed.'
segments.s666-g346: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a senior general). کدامند is an archaic/classical interrogative form 'which/which ones are they?' with the plural copula ending -ند. و simply connects the two questions. گردان here means 'regiment/division/troop.' کیند is an old interrogative form (کی + اند) meaning 'who are they' / 'who (are)'; functionally another plural copula question.
segments.s666-g347: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past used of a ruler = 'he commanded/said.' تا here introduces purpose or a subordinate clause ('that/so that'). نامه پندمند is a noun phrase (noun + adjectival modifier): 'a prudent/admonitory letter' (note classical word order: noun then modifier).
segments.s666-g348: نبشتند is a classical form used after تا to express the instructed action — effectively 'that they write' (older/poetic form corresponding to a subjunctive/optative). نزدیک means 'near' or 'close to.' آن is the demonstrative 'that.' پر here is the prefix/adjunct 'full (of)' and گزند means 'harm/danger,' so نزدیک آن پر گزند = 'near that place full of danger' or 'close to that peril.'
segments.s666-g349: یکی نامه = 'one letter' (numeric/indefinite 'one'). با + هدیه = 'with a gift,' and شاهوار is an adjective meaning 'royal/regal' modifying هدیه (adjective follows the noun in this ordering).
segments.s666-g350: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying the preceding gift. آن را uses the demonstrative + object marker to refer back to the gift. نشاید is an impersonal/modal classical form 'it is not fitting/it ought not,' followed by the infinitive گرفتن 'to take/accept.' شمار here means 'count' or 'consideration,' so the clause means roughly 'which ought not be taken/considered (as payment).' Note the classical ordering where a modal + infinitive conveys propriety/possibility.
segments.s666-g351: فرستاده را گفت = 'he said/told (to) the messenger,' with را marking the direct object (the messenger). سوی هری is the direction phrase 'toward Hēr(i)/Herat' (سوی + place). In classical narrative, گفت + direct object often introduces a command or reported speech.
segments.s666-g352: همی + رو is a classical verbal expression: همی is a prefatory/adverbial element signalling ongoing or immediate action, and رو here is the verb 'go' (imperative/infinitive sense 'go on'). چو = 'when/if,' پیدا = 'seen/appears,' شود is the auxiliary 'become/be (subjunctive/optative),' and لشکری = 'an army/host'; together: 'go toward Herat; when an army is sighted...' (the sentence leaves the condition open, typical of narrative command clauses).
segments.s666-g193: که is a relative/conjunction 'that' introducing a clause about the standard. در پیش رستم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of Rostam' (در + پیش + name; پیش here = 'before/in the presence of'). بدی روز جنگ is a classical adjective–noun sequence: بدی (an evaluative adjective 'ill/bad') modifies روز ('day'), and روز جنگ = 'day of battle'. Word order reflects older/poetic Persian where modifiers can precede the head.
segments.s666-g194: سبک here functions adverbially before the subject, meaning 'lightly' or 'gently'. شاه ایران is the subject 'the king of Iran'. گرفت is a simple past (3rd sg.) 'took/seized'. آن is the demonstrative object 'it' (the standard); the object follows the verb (classical/poetic order) and is emphasized by being post-verbal. به چنگ is an idiomatic phrase 'into the grasp' (به = 'towards/into', چنگ = 'claw/hand/grasp').
segments.s666-g195: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ببسود is an archaic/imprecisely inflected predicate meaning 'was of use/proved profitable' (impersonal/optative nuance in classical usage). خندان is a participial/adverbial form 'smiling' describing the manner; به بهرام داد = 'gave to Bahram' (به = 'to', داد = past 'gave'). Word order places the participle before the indirect object.
segments.s666-g196: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/plentifully' modifying the verb phrase. بر او آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (noun 'praise/acclamation') with کرد ('made/did') yields 'he praised/acclaimed him'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on' attaching to the pronoun او ('him'). یاد at the end functions as a noun 'remembrance' or 'in memory', an adjunct completing the sense 'he praised him much in remembrance'.
segments.s666-g197: به بهرام گفت = 'he said/told to Bahram' (به + name + گفت). آنک is the conjunction 'that' introducing reported speech. جدان is an adverb 'apart/aside' (classical usage) modifying the following verb; من is the first‑person pronoun 'me/I' placed before the verb (classical word order), while the actual object clitic may appear on the verb in the next segment.
segments.s666-g198: همی‌خواندندش is a past progressive plural form 'they used to recite/sing it' — همی is the archaic progressive marker and the enclitic -ش marks the third‑person object 'it/him'. سر انجمن means 'at the head of the assembly' or 'before the assembly' (سر + انجمن, classical juxtaposition used for 'in the assembly'). The verb carries both the progressive sense and the object clitic; the phrase as a whole reports habitual recital in gatherings.
segments.s666-g199: کجا here functions rhetorically/exclamatorily (classical 'what/how' or 'what a...'), not a literal locative. نام او = 'his name' (noun + possessive pronoun). رستم پهلوان is apposition: the proper name followed by the title/epithet 'pahlavān' ('champion'); in classical style titles often follow the name.
segments.s666-g200: This is a string of epithets joined by و ('and'): جهانگیر (world‑conquering), پیروز (victorious), روشن‌روان (bright‑souled). They are adjectival/appositive attributes of the named hero; no finite verb is present because the line is an exclamatory nominal description.
segments.s666-g249: چنین باد is an optative formula: چنين 'thus/so' + باد (optative particle) = 'may it be so.' The following وَ هرگز مبادا 'and never may it be' uses هرگز (never) + مبادا (negative optative: 'may it not be/let it not happen'), and جز این 'except this' sets up the exception that is then given by the next clause.
segments.s666-g250: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/except that'). The clause has the pronoun او 'he' as subject, شهریاری 'sovereignty/kingship' as predicate noun, and شود 'becomes' as the verbal predicate (third-person singular past/present-subjunctive sense used here to mean 'become'). بافرین is an epithet after the verb meaning 'praiseworthy/with praise' modifying the resulting sovereignty.
segments.s666-g251: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' The phrase follows standard classical order: subject موبد (the mobad/priest), source phrase ز شاه 'from the king' (ز is the poetic form of از), object این سخن‌ها 'these words,' and the verb شنید 'heard' at the end (simple past).
segments.s666-g252: بپژمرد is a past-form verb ('he withered/grew faint') with the archaic prefix بـ often found in epic diction for verbal forms. Then 'و لب را بدندان گزید' is coordinate: لب را (lip + definite object marker را) is the object; بدندان 'with the teeth' is an instrumental/adverbial phrase; گزید is past 'bit' (here 'to bite').
segments.s666-g253: همی‌داشت is an imperfect/continuous past ('used to have/kept') formed with the archaic continuous particle همی + داشت. اندر دل این شهریار is a locative/possessive phrase 'in the heart of this king' (اندر = in, دل = heart, این شهریار = this king), so the verb describes an ongoing state located in the king's heart.
segments.s666-g254: چنین 'thus' links back to the content kept in the heart. تا is the temporal 'until,' برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared' (i.e. 'came about'), and برین روزگار is a unit meaning 'that time/aforementioned time' — برین is an archaic/poetic form (بر + ین/این) modifying روزگار (time/fate).
segments.s666-g255: ز درگه 'from the court' uses the poetic preposition ز (from). یکی 'a certain' qualifies راز‌داری; here راز‌داری functions as 'a keeper of secrets/confidant' (a trusted man). بجست is a past verb 'sprang/leapt forward' (classical diction for 'stepped forward' or 'came forward').
segments.s666-g256: که تا forms a purpose clause 'so that/in order that.' این سخن is the object 'these words,' بازجوید is a subjunctive/purpose verb meaning 'may inquire/investigate' (بازجویدن = to question/investigate), and درست is an adverb/adjective 'correctly/properly' qualifying the investigation.
segments.s666-g97: به لشکر is the indirect object ('to the army') placed before the verb for emphasis. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' with گفت as the past-tense verb 'said'; پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. پهلوان (the subject) follows the verb due to the poetic inverted word order.
segments.s666-g98: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/attention particle (poetic), effectively 'O'. نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'renowned ones' and روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled' describing those being addressed.
segments.s666-g99: کم‌آزار and کم‌زیان are adjectival compounds meaning 'of little harm' / 'harmless' and 'of small loss' respectively. باشید is the polite/plural imperative 'be' addressed to the addressees; و هم links the two qualities ('and also').
segments.s666-g100: بدی را is the definite object ('the harm/evil' marked by را). مبندید here is a prohibitory imperative sense 'do not bind/bring together' (poetic/older form), with هرگز 'never' reinforcing the prohibition and میان 'between' indicating among yourselves.
segments.s666-g101: چو خواهید is a conditional 'if you wish' (چو = 'if/when', خواهید = 'you wish'). کایزد is an optative/vocative invoking God ('O God' / 'may God'), and بود یارتان is a formulaic clause 'may (he) be your ally' with بود as the copula and یارتان 'your companion/ally'.
segments.s666-g102: کند is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive used optatively 'may (he) make'. روشن is the result predicate 'bright/clear', and این تیره بازارتان ('this dark market of yours') is the object phrase (این='this', تیره='dark', بازارتان='your market'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s666-g103: شب تیره is the noun plus adjective 'the dark night'. چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ناله کرنای is a genitive construction 'the wailing/cry of the trumpet' (ناله = wail, کرنای = trumpet blast).
segments.s666-g104: برآمد is the past/punctual verb 'arose/issued forth'. بجنبید is a perfective/past plural sense 'they were roused / they moved', and یک‌سر ز جای is an adverbial phrase 'all at once from their places' (یک‌سر = 'at once/completely', ز = 'from', جای = 'place').
segments.s666-g105: بران گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner' (بران = 'upon/thereon', گونه = 'manner/way') placed before the verb for emphasis. رانید is a past/past-poetic form of راند (to drive) 'he drove (away)'. یک‌سر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'at once/straightaway'. ستور is the direct object 'horses/steeds' following the verb — typical flexible word order in classical Persian.
segments.s666-g106: که‌گر is a conditional 'if' (classical compound = که + گر). خیزد is the present/subjunctive-form of خاست used here with a conditional sense 'should rise / should start up'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and شب تیره is 'the dark night' (تیره = dark). هور is a proper name/epithet placed after the noun phrase (here read as the name Hūr), so اندر شب تیره هور = 'in the dark night of Hūr'.
segments.s666-g107: ز here functions as a causal preposition 'because of'. The phrase lists two paired qualities: نیروی (strength) و آسودگی (ease/rest) and then names the bearers of those qualities اسب و مرد (horse and man). Word order groups qualities then the nouns they qualify — a common compact classical structure.
segments.s666-g108: نیندیشد is a past negative 'he did not think' (third-person singular). از is the preposition 'about/concerning' (here 'of/regarding'), روزگار is 'time/occasion' and نبرد is 'battle' — the phrase از روزگار نبرد means 'of the time/occasion of battle' or more idiomatically 'that there would be battle'.
segments.s666-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی ('information/news') is the subject; آمد is the past tense 'came'. بر with a person (بر شهریار) marks the recipient 'to the king' — literally 'information came to the king'.
segments.s666-g110: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. داننده is an epithet 'knower/one who knows' modifying the name بهرام (Bahram). چون here is used as an interrogative/relative 'how' ('how he did it'), followed by ساخت (past 'made/arranged') and کار 'the affair' as the object. The clause gives the information reported to the king.
segments.s666-g111: ز here again means 'because of' or 'on account of'. گفتار و کردار او is the genitive/attributive phrase 'his words and deeds'. گشت is the past of گشتن used copularly 'he became' and شاد is the resulting adjective 'glad' — together 'because of his words and deeds he became glad.'
segments.s666-g112: در گنج is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the treasury/treasure-house'. بگشاد is the classical past form (بـ- prefix + گشاد) functioning as a past verb 'he opened'. The conjunction و links the second action: روزی (here 'provisions / livelihood') is the object and بداد is a past verb 'he gave' (classical past of دادن), so و روزی بداد = 'and he gave provisions.'
segments.s666-g233: ازان پس = archaic compound (fromِ + آنِ + پس) meaning 'after that' or 'from that time on'; چه گویی is an interrogative phrase 'what do you say' where چه (interrogative 'what') precedes the verb; گویی here is the present/imperative-type form of گفتن 'to say' used in direct address (classical ordering: question‑word + verb).
segments.s666-g234: همه داستان‌ها بباید زدن: همه داستان‌ها is the direct object 'all the stories/tales'; بباید + زدن is a periphrastic modal construction where بباید (must/ought) governs the infinitive زدن 'to strike/beat' — word order places the object before the modal + infinitive (typical Persian/Persian epic economy). The clause reads 'one must strike (or smite) the body of all the tales.'
segments.s666-g235: بدو گفت موبد = بدو ('to him') + گفت (said) + موبد (the priest). بدو is the unstressed prepositional form for به + او 'to him'; word order is indirect‑object + verb + subject (classic poetic inversion).
segments.s666-g236: که جاوید زی که خود جاودان زندگی را سزی functions as reported speech/exhortation: که introduces the clause; جاوید زی is an imperative/exhortative phrase 'be/ live eternal' (جاوید = 'eternal', زی = archaic imperative 'live'); the following clause (که خود ... سزی) gives a reason or qualification — خود 'yourself', جاودان زندگی را 'an immortal life' (را marks the definite object), and سزی is an archaic verbal/adjectival root meaning 'befit/deserve' (here 'you deserve').
segments.s666-g237: بدین برز و بالای این پهلوان is a prepositional/adjectival phrase: بدین (به + این in older orthography) means 'to/with this' or 'by this'; برز و بالا means 'height/exaltation and loftiness' (paired nouns used poetically), followed by این پهلوان 'this champion/hero' as the referent. The order emphasizes the lofty rank ('to this height and loftiness of the hero').
segments.s666-g238: بدین تیزگفتار روشن‌روان is another adjectival/ablative phrase modifying the hero: بدین again 'with this/so', تیزگفتار 'sharp‑tongued' (an adjective/epithet), روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled' (another epithet). The stacked epithets are typical of epic diction, linked by implicit coordination.
segments.s666-g239: نباشد مگر شاد و پیروزگر: نباشد is an impersonal/negative present/subjunctive 'there is not / there will not be' (classical usage), مگر = 'except/excepting', so the clause means 'there will be none except (those who are) glad and triumphant.' شاد و پیروزگر are predicative adjectives 'happy and victorious.'
segments.s666-g240: وزو دشمن شاه زیر و زبر: وزو is an older/conjoined form meaning 'and from him/by him' (و + زو/از او), introducing the agent; دشمن شاه 'the king's enemy' is the object; زیر و زبر is an idiomatic pair meaning 'upside down / overturned' (زیر = 'under', زبر = 'over'), together describing the enemy's being overthrown. Word order places the agent phrase before the affected noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s666-g393: 'گر' is a conditional particle here meaning 'if'. 'آید' is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form of آمد used in classical Persian to express a hypothetical or potential event ('should come' / 'comes'). 'بزشتی گمانی' is a noun phrase 'an ugly suspicion' (adjective + noun), and 'مبر' is the negative imperative 'do not take/accept' directed at the addressee; together the clause means 'If an ugly suspicion arises, do not accept it.'
segments.s666-g394: The particle 'که' here gives a causal/explanatory clause ('for' / 'because'). 'این مرزبانی بود' is a nominal clause with 'بود' as the past copula ('this was a border-guard/post'), and 'بر گذر' is a prepositional phrase 'on the pass/checkpoint' (بر + گذر). Word order is typical classical Persian: demonstrative + predicate + copula, then locative.
segments.s666-g395: 'وگر' = 'and if' / 'but if' introduces a second conditional possibility. 'زینهاری' (literally 'by way of warning') qualifies the following noun phrase; it is used adverbially here. 'یکی نامجوی' = 'a certain name-seeker' (یکی = 'a certain/some', نامجوی = 'one who seeks a name/fame' or a petitioner), so the whole segment means 'and if, as a warning, some name-seeking man...'.
segments.s666-g396: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'کشور' = 'the country/realm' is the source phrase. 'سوی شاه' is a directional phrase 'toward the king' (سوی = toward). 'بنهاد' is a classical perfective/preterite-type verb meaning 'placed/put' or idiomatically 'turned/laid' (here part of the idiom with 'روی'); 'روی' is 'face' and with بنهاد it yields the idiom 'to turn one's face (toward)'. The literal order is Source + Direction + Verb + Object (face).
segments.s666-g397: 'ور‌ای' is a compound of the conditional/alternative particle 'ور' (if/or) plus the vocative 'ای' ('O'), so 'Or, O...'. 'دون' is an epithet meaning 'low' or 'base'. 'که‌ه' (classical variant of 'که') introduces a relative/explanatory phrase 'which/that'. 'بازارگانی سپاه' places the collective notion of merchants (بازارگانی) together with سپاه 'host/army'—i.e. a caravan or mass of merchants described as a 'host' — so the segment describes 'a mean man, namely a merchant caravan/contingent.'
segments.s666-g398: 'بیاورد' is the present/subjunctive or modal 3rd-person form of 'آورد' meaning 'brings' or 'lets bring'. 'تا' introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning 'so that'. 'باشد' is the subjunctive/potential copula 'may be' / 'let it be'. 'ایمن' is an adjective 'safe'. 'به راه' is the locative phrase 'on/to the road'. Together the clause reads 'let him bring (it) so that the road may be safe.'
segments.s666-g399: The initial 'که باشد' repeats a purposive/subjunctive sense 'that it may be / so that'. The second 'که' introduces the content clause 'that he bring...' ('آرد' = 'brings' here). 'بروی تو روی' is an idiomatic phrase: 'بروی' (from رفت/رو) 'to go/come' combined with 'روی' 'face' and the possessor 'تو' 'you' — together 'bring his face before you' = 'come and present himself to you' or 'face you'. Word order is subordinate clause with verb in classical form placed before the object.
segments.s666-g400: The concessive 'وگر' = 'and if / but if' opens a contrasting hypothetical. 'کوه و دریا' are simple nouns 'mountain and sea'. 'شود' is the present/subjunctive of شدن 'become' ('should become' / 'becomes'). 'کینه جوی' is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'seeker of hatred/revenge' (کینه = grudge/hatred, جوی = seeking/searching). Together: 'and if even mountain and sea become vengeance-seekers.'
segments.s666-g209: پراگنده is an adjective meaning “scattered” placed before the verb. گشتند is the past‑tense, 3rd person plural of گشتن used here to mean “became / were made” (they became scattered). گردان شاه is a genitive phrase ’the king’s corps/units’ with the ezāfe omitted (poetic compression) so گردان is the subject and شاه qualifies it.
segments.s666-g210: همان is an emphatic demonstrative here (‘that very / the same’) introducing the next noun phrase. شادمان is an adjectival/nominal modifier meaning ‘rejoicing’ placed before پهلوان (typical Persian adjective–noun order). پهلوان سپاه is a possessive construction ‘champion/hero of the host’ with the ezāfe omitted for brevity; the whole phrase reads ‘that very rejoicing champion of the army/host.’
segments.s666-g211: سپیده is the subject ‘dawn.’ چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ برزد is an archaic past form used poetically with سر to mean ‘raised/shot up’; grammatically it is a past intransitive verb (here ‘rose / lifted’). سر ‘head’ means summit/top (the idiom “سر برزد” = ‘raise its head’). از کوه بر places the action spatially ‘from the mountain, over the mountain,’ with بر functioning adverbially to mean ‘over/above.’
segments.s666-g212: پدید آمد is a two-part verbal expression meaning ‘appeared / came into view’ (پدید + آمد). آن is a demonstrative ‘that’ pointing to the following noun. زرد and رخشان are stacked adjectives ‘yellow’ and ‘radiant/shining’ that precede and modify سپر (‘shield’) in typical Persian order, yielding ‘that yellow, shining shield.’
segments.s666-g213: سپهبد is the subject (a military title). بیامد is the simple past ‘came.’ بایوان is an archaic/poetic form (here functioning like ایوان, ‘iwan/portico’) and is used with شاه (‘the king’) as a genitive pair; again the linking ezāfe is omitted in this compressed poetic syntax.
segments.s666-g214: بکش کرده is a participial construction: بکش (from کشیدن ‘to draw/pull’) in a past participle-like use plus کرده ‘having done/made’ yields ‘having drawn’ or ‘with (it) drawn.’ دست is the noun ‘hand.’ اندر آن بارگاه is a locative phrase ‘into/within that court/palace’ (اندر = ‘in/within’). Grammatically this whole chunk describes attendant circumstance: ‘having drawn his hand, (he) entered that court.’
segments.s666-g215: بدو گفت is the inverted order for ‘he said to him’ (بدو = ‘to him,’ followed by گفت ‘said’). من بی‌بهانه شدم is a first‑person statement: من ‘I,’ بی‌بهانه ‘without cause / unjustly,’ شدم the past‑form of شدن ‘I became / I was’ — here idiomatically ‘I was wronged / I have come to be without cause.’
segments.s666-g216: بفر is a short imperative/optative form meaning ‘send’ or ‘bestow/offer’ (context determines nuance). تو is the vocative/second‑person ‘you.’ تاج زمانه is a compound noun ‘the crown of the age/realm’ (تاج ‘crown,’ زمانه ‘age/realm’), and شدم again is the past of شدن; here the verbal sequence is elliptical/poetic — the speaker asks the addressee to grant or send the crown, with شدم functioning as part of the clause’s predicate (poetic compression makes the exact syntactic links compact).
segments.s666-g337: به دل بر چو اندیشه بسیار گشت is a compact clause meaning 'when many thoughts came upon the heart.' چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'; به دل بر (= بر دل) is a locative phrase 'upon/into the heart' placed before چو for metrical/poetic order; اندیشه بسیار گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) in the sense 'became/occurred' — literally 'thoughts became many' = 'many thoughts arose.'
segments.s666-g338: ز بهرام پر‌درد و تیمار گشت: ز here functions causally 'because of/through Bahram'; the clause predicates a change with گشت 'became.' پر‌درد is an adjectival compound 'full of pain,' followed by و تیمار 'and (full of) care/anxiety.' The subject is carried over from the previous clause (he became...).
segments.s666-g339: روانش پر از غم دلش به دو نیم is a piled description: روانش پر از غم = 'his soul/spirit was full of grief' (پر after the noun means 'full (of)'). دلش به دو نیم = 'his heart into two halves' (i.e. split); the pronoun -ش attaches to both روان and دل marking possession 'his.'
segments.s666-g340: همی‌داشتی زان به دل ترس و بیم: همی‌داشتی is an imperfect/past-continuous form meaning 'he had/was bearing' (an archaic/poetic verbal form); زان 'from that/therefore' links to the prior clause; به دل here marks location 'in his heart,' and ترس و بیم are coordinated nouns 'fear and dread/anxiety.' Together: 'from that his heart bore fear and dread.'
segments.s666-g341: شب تیره بر‌زد سر از برج ماه: the noun + adjective order 'شب تیره' = 'dark night.' سر برزد (literally 'struck/lifted its head') is a poetic idiom meaning 'raised its head' or 'showed itself'; از برج ماه = 'from the tower of the moon' (locator). The verb برزد is past-tense/poetic.
segments.s666-g342: بخراد برزین چنین گفت شاه is inverted word order for reported speech: two proper names (here vocative/recipients) 'بخراد، برزین' followed by چنین 'thus' and گفت شاه 'the king said.' In this construction the verb گفت (said) comes after the addressees and manner word, typical of epic style.
segments.s666-g343: که بر‌ساز تا سوی دشمن شوی: که + تا introduce purpose/result 'that/so that'; بر‌ساز is an imperative/jussive 'make ready/prepare' (بر + ساز), سوی دشمن 'toward the enemy' is the direction, and شوی is subjunctive/optative 'you may become/go' — together: 'prepare so that you may go against the enemy.'
segments.s666-g344: بکوشی و ز تاختن نغنوی: بکوشی is an exhortation 'strive!' (2nd person subjunctive/imperative); و links the clauses; ز تاختن is a prepositional gerund phrase 'from/with charging' (تاختن = 'to charge'); نغنوی is a negative/poetic form of غنود (sleep/slumber/abate) — here meaning 'do not slumber/shirk' (i.e. do not withhold from charging).
segments.s666-g201: درفش ویست is a nominal statement 'the banner/standard is this' (ویست = archaic/poetic 'is'). این‌که introduces a following clause (a content/relative clause). داری is 2nd‑person present 'you have/hold' and بدست is a locative phrase 'in the hand' attached to that verb. Word order: main noun + copula + clause (این‌که + verb + complement).
segments.s666-g202: که here introduces a purpose/optative clause ('so that' / 'that'), not a relative. پیروز is an adjective 'victorious'. بادی is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person subjunctive/optative of بودن (≈ 'may you be' / 'you should be'); this verbal sense is not listed in the provided senses. و links the coordinated predicate and خسروپرست is a predicative noun/adjective meaning 'one who reveres/serves the king'.
segments.s666-g203: گمانم is 'I suppose / I think' with the first‑person clitic (‑م). که introduces the subordinate clause. In the clause the copula is omitted (typical in Persian), so تو رستم دیگری reads 'you [are] another Rostam' with تو as subject and دیگری as predicative.
segments.s666-g204: به marks the domain/measure in which the comparison holds ('in/with regard to'); a series of abstract nouns follows joined by و. مردی here means 'manliness' (quality), گردی is an older/poetic word (sense not supplied in the list) and فرمانبری means 'obedience' or 'submission'. Syntactically this whole prepositional phrase modifies 'رستم دیگری' ('another Rostam in manliness, courage, and obedience').
segments.s666-g205: برو آفرین here functions as an exclamatory expression ('Bravo!' / 'well done!'). کرد is the past tense verb 'he did/said' making an utterance. پس is 'then/afterwards' and پهلوان (postposed) is the subject 'the champion/hero' who utters the exclamation. Word order places the verb of utterance (کرد) before the subject, a common poetic variation.
segments.s666-g206: که introduces the content of the utterance (a hortatory/optative clause). پیروزگر is an adjective/noun 'triumphant, conqueror'. باش is the imperative/optative form 'be!' (here 'may you be' / 'be thou'). و links the coordinated predicate and روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑soul' / 'cheerful of spirit' completing the benediction.
segments.s666-g207: ز میدان بیامد is a simple past action 'he came from the field' (ز = 'from', میدان = 'the field/arena', بیامد = 'came'). بجای نشست is a compact expression 'sat in (his) place / took his seat' (بجای = 'in the place', نشست = past 'sat/took seat'), forming a single sequenced action.
segments.s666-g208: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / general'. درفش تهمتن is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the banner of Tahmatan' (adjacent nouns form the genitive relationship), and بدست is an adverbial 'in hand' indicating that he holds that banner. Together: 'the spahbed, Tahmatan's banner in hand.'
segments.s666-g313: سواری ('rider') is the noun subject placed before the verb for emphasis. بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' (classical form bi-âmad). خرید here is a verbal-noun/infinitival sense 'to buy' (action 'buying'), so the phrase means 'came to buy'; آن جوال is the direct object phrase 'that bag' following the infinitive.
segments.s666-g314: ندادش is the past negative transitive verb 'did not give' with the enclitic -ش 'it/him/her' referring to the bag. بها is the noun 'price/value' (the thing not given). و is the coordinating 'and'. بپیچید is past tense 'he turned/whirled' (prefix بـ plus پیچید), and یال ('mane') is the object of that action — poetically 'he turned his horse's mane' i.e. he turned and rode off.
segments.s666-g315: خروشان is an adverb/participle 'roaring' or 'with a roar', modifying the verb. بیامد is past 'came'. ببهرام is the prepositional phrase 'to/for Bahram' (be + Bahram). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used to introduce the following reported clause).
segments.s666-g316: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech. کاهست is the copular/stative phrase (here meaning 'it is hay/straw' in older/poetic spelling); لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while'. مرا contains the object 'me' (split in the annotation into م + را), with را marking it as definite object. در is the preposition 'in/inside'. نهفت is the past verb 'hid/concealed' (he hid me inside it). Word order follows poetic flexibility: the object (مرا را) appears before the verb نهفت.
segments.s666-g317: بهای جوالی is a noun phrase 'the price/value of the bag' (بهای = 'price/value', جوالی = modifier 'of the bag' in elliptical/genitive relation). همی‌داشتم is an imperfect/classical past-continuous form 'I had / I was possessing' (here simple past meaning 'I had'). The verb follows the noun phrase, as is common in Persian.
segments.s666-g318: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before / in the presence of' (به + پیش). سپاه is 'army/host'. تو is the 2nd-person 'your' (addressing Bahram). بگذاشتم is past first-person 'I placed / I put' (بـ- prefix plus گذاشتم). The full clause thus expresses 'I placed (it) before your army.'
segments.s666-g319: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. بستد is an older/powerful past-form verb here meaning 'seized/intercepted/closed upon' (classical usage; the exact nuance is context-dependent). از من is the prepositional phrase 'from me' (i.e. taking something away from me), سواری is 'a rider/horseman' (the agent), and به راه is the locative 'on the road' (adverbial). Word order presents the verb early for poemmatic emphasis.
segments.s666-g320: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which' referring back to the rider. دارد is present/past-stative 'has/was wearing' (classical auxiliary/possession). به سر 'on (his) head' (به = on/toward, سر = head). بر ز آهن کلاه is a descriptive phrase 'a helmet of iron' (بر ز = on/of/from iron; کلاه = helmet/hat). Together: 'who has on his head an iron helmet.'
segments.s666-g281: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial ('after that'); چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). کامِ دل is a possessive noun phrase 'heart's desire' (کام = wish, دل = heart) and آرد is a third-person narrative verb 'brings/comes' (classical/poetic present used in past narrative). بمشت is an instrumental/adjunct phrase (literally 'with a fist' in context) that modifies the following action.
segments.s666-g282: بپیچد is a third-person verb (prefix ب- + present/stative form) meaning 'turn/twist' used in narrative; سر (the noun) is the direct theme 'head' and is followed by the prepositional phrase از شاه 'away/from the king'. و گردَد درشت links the next predicate: گردد (گشت) 'become' and درشت 'coarse/rude' is a predicative adjective meaning 'becomes rude'.
segments.s666-g283: بیامد is a past narrative verb 'came'; بر شاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' (بر = toward/to). این را بگفت is a verb + object construction: این = 'this', را is the definite/direct-object marker, and بگفت ('said/told') is the reported past-tense verb.
segments.s666-g284: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/ruler'; با introduces accompaniment ('with'); درد و غم are nouns 'pain and sorrow' (emotional suffering). گشت is the past of 'to become' or 'was turned', and جفت is a predicative noun meaning 'paired/joined' (i.e., he became paired with/attended by pain and sorrow).
segments.s666-g285: ورا is a dative/indirect-object pronoun 'to him'; آن سخن = 'that speech/news'. بتر آمد is a comparative idiom: بتر (more bad/worse) + آمد ('came/seemed') meaning 'appeared worse', and ز مرگ is the standard comparative marker 'than death' (ز = 'than/from' in poetic usage).
segments.s666-g286: بپژمرد is a verb 'withered' (past narrative form). و شد links the second result clause: شد 'became' and تیره is the predicate 'dark/ominous' describing آن سبز برگ 'that green leaf' (سبز برگ = 'green leaf'), so the clause states that the leaf withered and turned dark.
segments.s666-g287: فرستاده‌ای here is a participial/noun phrase 'a messenger' (literally 'one sent') and خواست is the past verb 'asked/ordered/requested' (i.e., 'sent for'). از در جوان is a prepositional/source phrase 'from/at the young man's door/court' (در can mean 'gate/house/court' here).
segments.s666-g288: فرستاد is the past verb 'sent/dispatched'. تازان is here an object (likely a group name or epithet) and پس is temporal 'then/after that'; پهلوان is an appositive or role-label 'the champion' — together the phrase reads 'then he sent the champion Tazān' (word order: verb + object/name + temporal adverb + apposition).
segments.s666-g273: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one'; اختری is a poetic epithet 'star‑like / star‑bright' modifying that one. کرد is the simple past 'did/placed' (here the main narrative verb). زان سر is a demonstrative + noun 'that head' (زان = archaic demonstrative 'that/ from that'), and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on/to the road' indicating where the action was done. Word order is flexible: the verb کرد comes before the object phrase in this line but the whole clause is best read as 'a certain, star‑bright man did (place) that head on the road.'
segments.s666-g274: کزین سان is an archaic/prepositional phrase 'from this / in this manner' (کزین = 'from this', سان = 'so/like this'). ببرم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive (classical narrative use to express intent) meaning 'I will take / I shall carry'. سر ساوه شاه is a genitive chain 'the head of the king of Saveh' (head + place/name + title). Together the clause expresses the speaker's declared intention: 'in this way I will carry the head of the king of Saveh.'
segments.s666-g275: به پیش سپاهش is a locative phrase 'before / in front of his army' (به + پیش + سپاه + ـش = 'in front of his army'). به راه is another locative 'on the road'; here افگنم is an archaic first‑person form (classical subjunctive) of افکندن 'to cast/put', expressing the speaker's action/intention 'I will cast / I will place'. The two prepositional phrases set the scene 'in front of his army, on the road'.
segments.s666-g276: همه لشکرش را: همه 'all/the whole' + لشکرش 'his army' with the definite object marker را. بهم here means 'into disorder / into confusion' (adverbial), and بر‌زنم is a first‑person present/subjunctive (classical optative) from زدن 'to strike / to put in motion' used idiomatically as 'I will rout / scatter'. The clause therefore states the intended result: 'I will scatter his entire host into disorder.'
segments.s666-g277: فرستاده شاه is 'the king's messenger' (noun + possessor). چون آن بدید uses the subordinating چون 'when' (archaic/conjunctional here) + آن 'that' and بدید, the simple past 'he saw'. So the whole segment reads 'when the king's messenger saw that...'.
segments.s666-g278: پی here means 'after/then' or 'thereupon'; افگند is the past tense 'he cast/threw' (of افکندن). فالی is the object 'a divination/omen'. چنان 'so/thus' modifies the following clause, and چون سزید is a fixed idiomatic phrase 'as was fitting / as was deserved' (سزید here has the sense 'be fitting / be deserved'). Altogether: 'thereupon he cast a divination, and the omen was accordingly / as fitting.'
segments.s666-g279: چنین گفت is the narrative formula 'thus he said'. کین here is an archaic demonstrative equivalent to 'this/that' (not the modern noun 'vengeance'); مرد پیروزبخت is 'a fortunate/fortunate man' (mord = man, پیروزبخت = lucky/fortunate). So the clause introduces speech: 'Thus he said: "This fortunate man..."'.
segments.s666-g280: بیابد is a present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find/obtain' used here to state a future/result 'he will obtain/attain'. به فرجام means 'at the end / in the outcome', and زین رنج تخت is an ablative/genitive phrase 'a throne from this suffering' (زین = archaic 'from this', رنج = suffering, تخت = throne). The whole clause: 'he will attain, in the end, a throne from this hardship.'
segments.s666-g89: بگرداند is the third‑person singular narrative/present (the historical present) of گردانیدن with the prefix ب‑, meaning “he wheels/turns” (causative). The direct object اسب follows the verb in normal Persian order. و simply coordinates to the next clause; بگوید is another third‑person present/narrative form of گفتن used here to introduce what he utters. نژاد sits as the opening word of the content of what he says (it functions as the noun/head of the following clause rather than an independent clause).
segments.s666-g90: کند is the present/subjunctive/causative of کردن, meaning “he causes/makes.” بر دل جنگیان is a prepositional phrase = “upon the hearts of the warriors” (بر governs دل; جنگیان is the genitive/possessive group). جنگ یاد is a classical/genitive ordering where جنگ (battle) qualifies یاد (memory) = “memory of battle.” The whole clause means “he causes the memory of battle to be upon the warriors' hearts.”
segments.s666-g91: دگر = “another” or “other/else” introducing a different person. آنک is a classical demonstrative/relative here meaning “that/who” and links to the following apposition. بد نام is a compound adjective “bad‑named” = “ill‑famed” placed before the proper name; ایزدگشسب is the proper name (Izad‑Gushasp).
segments.s666-g92: کز is a relative particle meaning “who/whom/from which” that introduces the relative clause. آتش = “fire.” نه برگاشتی is a negated past/historical verb (نه = not; برگاشتن = to lift/uncover/turn away), so “did not turn/lift.” روی اسب = “the face of the horse” (روی = face; followed by اسب = horse indicating the possessor). The clause reads “who did not turn the horse's face away from the fire.”
segments.s666-g93: بفرمود is an honorific past form meaning “he commanded/said.” تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here (≈ “that/so that”). گوش دارد literally “has ear” = “is attentive” and in this line functions as a descriptive phrase; بنه is ambiguous in isolation (it can be a noun meaning a pistachio or a small bundle) but here most likely is a personal or unit name or the object/person whom the order concerns — the structure is classical: verb + تا + [descriptor + name].
segments.s666-g94: کند is the subordinate verb “should make/do” governed by the command. میسره and میمنه are archaic/military terms for the two flanks/sides (left and right); راست here means “straight/right” and با is the preposition “with.” The phrase میسره راست با میمنه therefore means “make/align the left straight with the right” — i.e., align the two wings. Note these military terms are archaic in modern Persian.
segments.s666-g95: به پشت سپه = “at the back/rear of the army” (به as locative/marker + پشت سپه). بود is the past copula “was.” همدان is the place name Hamadan and follows the verb as locative in this classical word order. گشسب (گشسب) is the subject/name (Gushasp).
segments.s666-g96: کجا = “where” (introducing the question). دم can mean “breath,” “moment,” or “tip/edge”; in this martial image it functions as a concrete part (≈ “tip/edge” or “tail”) of the lions. شیران = “lions” (literal or figurative for brave men). گرفتی is second‑person past “you seized/took.” به اسب = literally “to/with the horse” here indicating the instrument/place (i.e., ‘on your horse’ or ‘with your horse’). The whole is a rhetorical question: “Where did you seize the lions (by the tail/edge) while on your horse?”

sentences.s666-s29: When the renowned king issues the command, they made me the army's champion.
sentences.s666-s30: They departed — the alert, well-informed men — and then also went to the sovereign of the world.
sentences.s666-s31: Bahram's words were so many; he bestowed ten for each poet.
sentences.s666-s32: The emperor of Iran was glad at that; he was freed from the charge of that army.
sentences.s666-s129: He said to him, "Quick—go after the champion; see what you find and call it out to me."
sentences.s666-s130: The spokesman of the swift came up from behind; no one was aware of his deeds.
sentences.s666-s131: who was both a guide and a soothsayer, and who would announce the outcome of every affair in the original/old manner.
